subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Examine the continuity of the function {tex}f(x) = {x^3} + 2{x^2} - 1{/tex} at x = 1
question_html: Examine the continuity of the function {tex}f(x) = {x^3} + 2{x^2} - 1{/tex} at x = 1
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x) = {x^3} + 2{x^2} - 1{/tex} at x = 1 {tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {\left( {1 + h} \right)^3} + 2{\left( {1 +h} \right)^2} - 1 = 2{/tex} and {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {\left( {1 - h} \right)^3} + 2{\left( {1 - h} \right)^2} - 1 = 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x){/tex} and {tex}f(1) = 1 + 2 - 1 = 2{/tex} So, f(x) is continuous at x = 1.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x) = {x^3} + 2{x^2} - 1{/tex} at x = 1

{tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {\left( {1 + h} \right)^3} + 2{\left( {1 +h} \right)^2} - 1 = 2{/tex}

and {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {\left( {1 - h} \right)^3} + 2{\left( {1 - h} \right)^2} - 1 = 2{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x){/tex}

and {tex}f(1) = 1 + 2 - 1 = 2{/tex}

So, f(x) is continuous at x = 1.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {3 x+5, \text { if } x \geq 2} \\ {x^{2}, \text { if } x<2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 2
question_html:
Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points:
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {3 x+5, \text { if } x \geq 2} \\ {x^{2}, \text { if } x<2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 2
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {3 x+5, \text { if } x \geq 2} \\ {x^{2}, \text { if } x<2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 2 At x = 2, {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}}(x)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}(2-h)^{2}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(4+h^{2}-4 h\right)=4{/tex} And {tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}}(3 x+5){/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}[3(2+h)+5]=11{/tex} Since, LHL {tex}\neq{/tex} RHL at x = 2 So, f(x) is discontinous at x = 2
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {3 x+5, \text { if } x \geq 2} \\ {x^{2}, \text { if } x<2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 2
At x = 2, {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}}(x)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}(2-h)^{2}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(4+h^{2}-4 h\right)=4{/tex}
And {tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}}(3 x+5){/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}[3(2+h)+5]=11{/tex}
Since, LHL {tex}\neq{/tex} RHL at x = 2
So, f(x) is discontinous at x = 2

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{1-\cos 2 x}{x^{2}}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 5, & \text { if } x=0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} at x = 0
question_html:
Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points:
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{1-\cos 2 x}{x^{2}}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 5, & \text { if } x=0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} at x = 0
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{1-\cos 2 x}{x^{2}}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 5, & \text { if } x=0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} At x = 0 {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{1-\cos 2 x}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-\cos 2(0-h)}{(0-h)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-\cos 2 h}{h^{2}}[\because \cos (-\theta)=\cos \theta]{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-1+2 \sin ^{2} h}{h^{2}}\left[\because \cos 2 \theta=1-2 \sin ^{2} \theta\right]{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2(\sin h)^{2}}{(h)^{2}}\left[\because \lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin h}{h}=1\right]{/tex} = 2 {tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{1-\cos 2 x}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1-\cos 2(0+h)}{(0+h)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin ^{2} h}{h^{2}}=2\left[\because \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin h}{h}=1\right]{/tex} And f(0) = 0 Since, LHL = RHL {tex}\neq{/tex} f(0) Hence, f(x) is not continuous at x = 0
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{1-\cos 2 x}{x^{2}}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 5, & \text { if } x=0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex}
At x = 0 {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{1-\cos 2 x}{x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-\cos 2(0-h)}{(0-h)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-\cos 2 h}{h^{2}}[\because \cos (-\theta)=\cos \theta]{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1-1+2 \sin ^{2} h}{h^{2}}\left[\because \cos 2 \theta=1-2 \sin ^{2} \theta\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2(\sin h)^{2}}{(h)^{2}}\left[\because \lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin h}{h}=1\right]{/tex}
= 2
{tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{1-\cos 2 x}{x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1-\cos 2(0+h)}{(0+h)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin ^{2} h}{h^{2}}=2\left[\because \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin h}{h}=1\right]{/tex}
And f(0) = 0
Since, LHL = RHL {tex}\neq{/tex} f(0)
Hence, f(x) is not continuous at x = 0

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{2 x^{2}-3 x-2}{x-2},} & {\text { if } x \neq 2} \\ {5,} & {\text { if } x=2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 2
question_html:
Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points:
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{2 x^{2}-3 x-2}{x-2},} & {\text { if } x \neq 2} \\ {5,} & {\text { if } x=2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 2
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{2 x^{2}-3 x-2}{x-2},} & {\text { if } x \neq 2} \\ {5,} & {\text { if } x=2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 2 At x = 2 {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} \frac{2 x^{2}-3 x-2}{x-2}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2(2-h)^{2}-3(2-h)-2}{(2-h)-2}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{8+2 h^{2}-8 h-6+3 h-2}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 h^{2}-5 h}{-h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h(2 h-5)}{-h}=5{/tex} {tex}R HL=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} \frac{2 x^{2}-3 x-2}{x-2}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2(2+h)^{2}-3(2+h)-2}{(2+h)-2}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{8+2 h^{2}+8 h-6-3 h-2}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 h^{2}+5 h}{h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h(2 h+5)}{h}=5{/tex} and f(2) = 5 {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(2) So, f(x) is continuous at x = 2
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{2 x^{2}-3 x-2}{x-2},} & {\text { if } x \neq 2} \\ {5,} & {\text { if } x=2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 2
At x = 2 {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} \frac{2 x^{2}-3 x-2}{x-2}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2(2-h)^{2}-3(2-h)-2}{(2-h)-2}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{8+2 h^{2}-8 h-6+3 h-2}{-h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 h^{2}-5 h}{-h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h(2 h-5)}{-h}=5{/tex}
{tex}R HL=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} \frac{2 x^{2}-3 x-2}{x-2}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2(2+h)^{2}-3(2+h)-2}{(2+h)-2}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{8+2 h^{2}+8 h-6-3 h-2}{h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 h^{2}+5 h}{h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h(2 h+5)}{h}=5{/tex}
and f(2) = 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(2)
So, f(x) is continuous at x = 2

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}}{{2(x - 4)}}}&{if\;x \ne 4} \\ {0,}&{if\;x = 4} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} at x = 4
question_html:
Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points:
{tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}}{{2(x - 4)}}}&{if\;x \ne 4} \\ {0,}&{if\;x = 4} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} at x = 4
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{|x-4|}{2(x-4)},} & {\text { if } x \neq 4} \\ {0,} & {\text { if } x=4} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 4 At x = 4, {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 4^{-}} \frac{|x-4|}{2(x-4)}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{|4-h-4|}{2[(4-h)-4]}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{|0-h|}{(8-2 h-8)}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h}{-2 h}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} and f(4) = 0 {tex}\neq{/tex} LHL So, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 4
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} {\frac{|x-4|}{2(x-4)},} & {\text { if } x \neq 4} \\ {0,} & {\text { if } x=4} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 4
At x = 4, {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 4^{-}} \frac{|x-4|}{2(x-4)}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{|4-h-4|}{2[(4-h)-4]}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{|0-h|}{(8-2 h-8)}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h}{-2 h}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} and f(4) = 0 {tex}\neq{/tex} LHL
So, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 4

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\left| x \right|\cos \frac{1}{x},}&{if\;x \ne 0} \\ {0,}&{if\;x = 0} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} at x = 0
question_html:
Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points:
{tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\left| x \right|\cos \frac{1}{x},}&{if\;x \ne 0} \\ {0,}&{if\;x = 0} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} at x = 0
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} |x| \cos \frac{1}{x}, \text { if } & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & \text { if } x=0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} at x = 0 At x = 0 {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}}|x| \cos \frac{1}{x}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}|0-h| \cos \frac{1}{0-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} h \cos \left(\frac{-1}{h}\right){/tex} = 0 {tex}\times{/tex} [an oscillating number between -1 and 1] = 0 {tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}}|x| \cos \frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}|0+h| \cos \frac{1}{(0+h)}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} h \cos \frac{1}{h}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\times{/tex} [an oscillating number between -1 and 1] = 0 and f(0) = 0 Since, LHL = RHL = f(0) So, f(x) is continuous at x = 0
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} |x| \cos \frac{1}{x}, \text { if } & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & \text { if } x=0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} at x = 0
At x = 0 {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}}|x| \cos \frac{1}{x}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}|0-h| \cos \frac{1}{0-h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} h \cos \left(\frac{-1}{h}\right){/tex}
= 0 {tex}\times{/tex} [an oscillating number between -1 and 1] = 0
{tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}}|x| \cos \frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}|0+h| \cos \frac{1}{(0+h)}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} h \cos \frac{1}{h}{/tex}
= 0 {tex}\times{/tex} [an oscillating number between -1 and 1] = 0
and f(0) = 0
Since, LHL = RHL = f(0)
So, f(x) is continuous at x = 0

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {|x - a|\sin \frac{1}{{x - a}},}&{{\text{ if}}\;{\text{ }}x \ne 0} \\ {0,}&{{\text{ if }}\;x = a} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} at x = a
question_html:
Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points:
{tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {|x - a|\sin \frac{1}{{x - a}},}&{{\text{ if}}\;{\text{ }}x \ne 0} \\ {0,}&{{\text{ if }}\;x = a} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} at x = a
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{lr} {|x-a| \sin \frac{1}{x-a},} & {\text { if } x \neq 0} \\ {0,} & {\text { if } x=a} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = a At x = a, {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \infty}|x-a| \sin \frac{1}{x-a}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}|a-h-a| \sin \left(\frac{1}{a-h-a}\right){/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}-h \ \ \sin \left(\frac{1}{h}\right)[\because \sin (-\theta)=-\sin \theta]{/tex} = 0 {tex}\times{/tex} [an oscillating number between -1 and 1] = 0 {tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow a^{+}}|x-a| \sin \left(\frac{1}{x-a}\right){/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}|a+h-a| \sin \left(\frac{1}{a+h-a}\right)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} h \sin \frac{1}{h}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\times{/tex} [an oscillating number between -1 and 1] = 0 and f(a) = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(a) So, f(x) is continuous at x = a.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{lr} {|x-a| \sin \frac{1}{x-a},} & {\text { if } x \neq 0} \\ {0,} & {\text { if } x=a} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = a
At x = a, {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \infty}|x-a| \sin \frac{1}{x-a}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}|a-h-a| \sin \left(\frac{1}{a-h-a}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}-h \ \ \sin \left(\frac{1}{h}\right)[\because \sin (-\theta)=-\sin \theta]{/tex}
= 0 {tex}\times{/tex} [an oscillating number between -1 and 1] = 0
{tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow a^{+}}|x-a| \sin \left(\frac{1}{x-a}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}|a+h-a| \sin \left(\frac{1}{a+h-a}\right)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} h \sin \frac{1}{h}{/tex}
= 0 {tex}\times{/tex} [an oscillating number between -1 and 1] = 0
and f(a) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(a)
So, f(x) is continuous at x = a.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Determine the continuity of the following function at the indicated point. {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}}{{1 + {e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 0
question_html: Determine the continuity of the following function at the indicated point.  {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}}{{1 + {e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 0
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}}{{1 + {e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 0 At x = 0, {tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}}{{1 + {e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{e^{1/0 - h}}}}{{1 + {e^{1/0 - h}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0}} \frac{{{e^{ - 1/h}}}}{{1 + {e^{ - 1/h}}}} {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0 }} \frac{1}{{{e^{1/h}} + 1}} = \frac{1}{{{e^\infty } + 1}} = \frac{1}{{\infty + 1}}{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {e^\infty } = \infty } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{\infty} = 0{/tex} {tex}RHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{{e^{1/x}}}}{{1 + {e^{1/x}}}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{e^{1/0 + h}}}}{{1 + {e^{1/0 + h}}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{e^{1/h}}}}{{1 + {e^{1/h}}}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{{{e^{ - 1/h}} + 1}} = \frac{1}{{{e^{ - \infty }} + 1}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{0 + 1}} = 1\left[ {\because {e^{ - \infty }} = 0} \right]{/tex} Hence, {tex}LHL \ne RHL{/tex} at x = 0 So, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 0.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}}{{1 + {e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 0 

At x = 0, {tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}}{{1 + {e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{e^{1/0 - h}}}}{{1 + {e^{1/0 - h}}}}{/tex}

{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0}} \frac{{{e^{ - 1/h}}}}{{1 + {e^{ - 1/h}}}} {/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0 }} \frac{1}{{{e^{1/h}} + 1}} = \frac{1}{{{e^\infty } + 1}} = \frac{1}{{\infty + 1}}{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {e^\infty } = \infty } \right]{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{1}{\infty} = 0{/tex}

{tex}RHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{{e^{1/x}}}}{{1 + {e^{1/x}}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{e^{1/0 + h}}}}{{1 + {e^{1/0 + h}}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{e^{1/h}}}}{{1 + {e^{1/h}}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{1}{{{e^{ - 1/h}} + 1}} = \frac{1}{{{e^{ - \infty }} + 1}}{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{1}{{0 + 1}} = 1\left[ {\because {e^{ - \infty }} = 0} \right]{/tex}

Hence, {tex}LHL \ne RHL{/tex} at x = 0

So, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 0.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2},}&{{\text{if}}\;{\text{ }}0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {2{x^2} - 3x + \frac{3}{2},}&{{\text{if}}\;{\text{ }}1 < x \leq 2} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} at x = 1
question_html:
Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points:
{tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2},}&{{\text{if}}\;{\text{ }}0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {2{x^2} - 3x + \frac{3}{2},}&{{\text{if}}\;{\text{ }}1 < x \leq 2} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} at x = 1
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {\frac{x^{2}}{2}, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {2 x^{2}-3 x+\frac{3}{2}, \text { if } 1<x \leq 2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 1 At x = 1, {tex}LH L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{x^{2}}{2}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1-h)^{2}}{2} {/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1+h^{2}-2 h}{2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}}\left(2 x^{2}-3 x+\frac{3}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[2(1+h)^{2}-3(1+h)+\frac{3}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(2+2 h^{2}+4 h-3-3 h+\frac{3}{2}\right)=-1+\frac{3}{2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} And {tex}f(1)=\frac{1^{2}}{2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(1) Hence, f(x) is continuous at x = 1.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {\frac{x^{2}}{2}, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {2 x^{2}-3 x+\frac{3}{2}, \text { if } 1<x \leq 2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 1
At x = 1, {tex}LH L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{x^{2}}{2}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1-h)^{2}}{2} {/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1+h^{2}-2 h}{2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}}\left(2 x^{2}-3 x+\frac{3}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[2(1+h)^{2}-3(1+h)+\frac{3}{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(2+2 h^{2}+4 h-3-3 h+\frac{3}{2}\right)=-1+\frac{3}{2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
And {tex}f(1)=\frac{1^{2}}{2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(1)
Hence, f(x) is continuous at x = 1.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points: {tex}f(x)=|x|+|x-1|{/tex} at x = 1
question_html:
Find which of the functions is continuous or discontinuous at the indicated points:
{tex}f(x)=|x|+|x-1|{/tex} at x = 1
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x)=|x|+|x-1|{/tex} at x = 1 At x = 1, {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}}[|x|+|x-1|]{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}[|1-h|+|1-h-1|]=1+0=1{/tex} And {tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}}[|x|+|x-1|]{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow 0}[|1+h|+|1+h-1|]=1+0=1{/tex} and {tex}f(1)=|1|+|0|=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(1) Hence, f(x) is continuous at x = 1
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x)=|x|+|x-1|{/tex} at x = 1
At x = 1, {tex}L H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}}[|x|+|x-1|]{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}[|1-h|+|1-h-1|]=1+0=1{/tex}
And {tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}}[|x|+|x-1|]{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow 0}[|1+h|+|1+h-1|]=1+0=1{/tex}
and {tex}f(1)=|1|+|0|=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(1)
Hence, f(x) is continuous at x = 1

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 3x - 8,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant 5 \hfill \\ 2k,\,\,if\,\,x > 5 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 5.
question_html: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 3x - 8,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant 5 \hfill \\ 2k,\,\,if\,\,x > 5 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 5.
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 3x - 8,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant 5 \hfill \\ 2k,\,\,if\,\,x > 5 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 5. {tex}\therefore LHL = RHL = f(5){/tex} Now, {tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \left( {3x - 8} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {3\left( {5 - h} \right) - 8} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {15 - 3h - 8} \right] = 7{/tex} {tex}RHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ + }} 2k = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} 2k = 2k {/tex} Since LHL = RHL, therefore 2k = 7,which gives {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} k= {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 3x - 8,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant 5 \hfill \\ 2k,\,\,if\,\,x > 5 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} 

Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 5.

{tex}\therefore LHL = RHL = f(5){/tex}

Now, {tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \left( {3x - 8} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {3\left( {5 - h} \right) - 8} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {15 - 3h - 8} \right] = 7{/tex}

{tex}RHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ + }} 2k = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} 2k = 2k {/tex}

Since LHL = RHL, therefore 2k = 7,which gives{tex}{/tex}

{tex}{/tex}k={tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex}

question_text: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{2^{x + 2}} - 16}}{{{4^x} - 16}},\,\,if\,x \ne 2 \hfill \\ k,\,\,if\,\,x = 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 2.
question_html: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{2^{x + 2}} - 16}}{{{4^x} - 16}},\,\,if\,x \ne 2 \hfill \\ k,\,\,if\,\,x = 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 2.
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{2^{x + 2}} - 16}}{{{4^x} - 16}},\,\,if\,x \ne 2 \hfill \\ k,\,\,if\,\,x = 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 2. {tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim}\limits_{x\to{2}}f(x)=f(2){/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim} \limits_{x\to{2}}f(x){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{{2^x}{{.2}^2} - {2^4}}}{{{4^x} - {4^2}}} {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{4\left( {{2^x} - 4} \right)}}{{{{\left( {{2^x}} \right)}^2} - {{\left( 4 \right)}^2}}} {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{4}{{{2^x} + 4}} = \frac{4}{8} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} But f(2) = k {tex}\therefore k = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{2^{x + 2}} - 16}}{{{4^x} - 16}},\,\,if\,x \ne 2 \hfill \\ k,\,\,if\,\,x = 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}

Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 2.

{tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim}\limits_{x\to{2}}f(x)=f(2){/tex}

{tex}\mathop {\lim} \limits_{x\to{2}}f(x){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{{2^x}{{.2}^2} - {2^4}}}{{{4^x} - {4^2}}} {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{4\left( {{2^x} - 4} \right)}}{{{{\left( {{2^x}} \right)}^2} - {{\left( 4 \right)}^2}}} {/tex}{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{4}{{{2^x} + 4}} = \frac{4}{8} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

But f(2) = k 

{tex}\therefore k = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the value of k, for which f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + k x } - \sqrt { 1 - k x } } { x } , \text { if } - 1 \leq x < 0 } \\ { \frac { 2 x + 1 } { x - 1 } , \quad \text { if } 0 \leq x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
question_html: Find the value of k, for which f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + k x } - \sqrt { 1 - k x } } { x } , \text { if } - 1 \leq x < 0 } \\ { \frac { 2 x + 1 } { x - 1 } , \quad \text { if } 0 \leq x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
solution_text: According to the question,we are given that, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + k x } - \sqrt { 1 - k x } } { x } , \text { if } - 1 \leq x < 0 } \\ { \frac { 2 x + 1 } { x - 1 } , \quad \text { if } 0 \leq x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0 and we have to find the value of k. Now, f(0) = {tex}\frac { 2(0) + 1 } { 0 - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { - 1 }{/tex} =-1 and LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - k h } - \sqrt { 1 + k h } } { - h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - k h } - \sqrt { 1 + k h } } { - h } \times \frac { ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) } { ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - k h ) - ( 1 + k h ) } { - h ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex} [(a + b)(a - b) = a 2 - b 2 ] {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { - 2 k h } { - h ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 k } { \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } } = \frac { 2 k } { 1 + 1 } = \frac { 2 k } { 2 }{/tex} = 2 Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 0. {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(0) = LHL {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 1 = k {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} k = -1
solution_html:
According to the question,we are given that, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + k x } - \sqrt { 1 - k x } } { x } , \text { if } - 1 \leq x < 0 } \\ { \frac { 2 x + 1 } { x - 1 } , \quad \text { if } 0 \leq x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0

and we have to find the value of k.
Now, f(0) = {tex}\frac { 2(0) + 1 } { 0 - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { - 1 }{/tex} =-1
and LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - k h } - \sqrt { 1 + k h } } { - h }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - k h } - \sqrt { 1 + k h } } { - h } \times \frac { ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) } { ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - k h ) - ( 1 + k h ) } { - h ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex} [(a + b)(a - b) = a2 - b2]
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { - 2 k h } { - h ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 k } { \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } } = \frac { 2 k } { 1 + 1 } = \frac { 2 k } { 2 }{/tex}= 2
Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 0.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(0) = LHL {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 1 = k
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} k = -1

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos kx}}{{x\sin x}},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ \frac{1}{2},\,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 0
question_html: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos kx}}{{x\sin x}},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ \frac{1}{2},\,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 0
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos kx}}{{x\sin x}},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ \frac{1}{2},\,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} At x = 0, {tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{1 - \cos kx}}{{x\sin x}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos k\left( {0 - h} \right)}}{{\left( {0 - h} \right)\sin \left( {0 - h} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h\to {0}} \frac{{1 - \cos \left( { - kh} \right)}}{{ - h\sin \left( { - h} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos kh}}{{ h\sin \,h}}\left[ {\because \cos \left( { - \theta } \right) = \cos \theta ,\sin \left( { - \theta } \right) = - \sin \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{1 - 1 + 2{{\sin }^2}\frac{{kh}}{2}}}{{h\sin \,h}}\left[ {\because \cos \theta = 1 - 2{{\sin }^2}\frac{\theta }{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}\frac{{kh}}{2}}}{{h\sin \,h}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{2{{\sin }}\frac{{kh}}{2}}}{{\frac{{kh}}{2}}}.\frac{{\sin \frac{{kh}}{2}}}{{\frac{{kh}}{2}}}.\frac{1}{{\frac{{\sin \,h}}{h}}}.\frac{{{k^2}h/4}}{h}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{2{k^2}}}{4} = \frac{{{k^2}}}{2}\left[ {\because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\sinh }}{h} = 1} \right]{/tex} Also, {tex}f(0) = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{{{k^2}}}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow k = \pm 1{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos kx}}{{x\sin x}},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ \frac{1}{2},\,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} 

At x = 0, {tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{1 - \cos kx}}{{x\sin x}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos k\left( {0 - h} \right)}}{{\left( {0 - h} \right)\sin \left( {0 - h} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h\to {0}} \frac{{1 - \cos \left( { - kh} \right)}}{{ - h\sin \left( { - h} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos kh}}{{ h\sin \,h}}\left[ {\because \cos \left( { - \theta } \right) = \cos \theta ,\sin \left( { - \theta } \right) = - \sin \theta } \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{1 - 1 + 2{{\sin }^2}\frac{{kh}}{2}}}{{h\sin \,h}}\left[ {\because \cos \theta = 1 - 2{{\sin }^2}\frac{\theta }{2}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}\frac{{kh}}{2}}}{{h\sin \,h}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{2{{\sin }}\frac{{kh}}{2}}}{{\frac{{kh}}{2}}}.\frac{{\sin \frac{{kh}}{2}}}{{\frac{{kh}}{2}}}.\frac{1}{{\frac{{\sin \,h}}{h}}}.\frac{{{k^2}h/4}}{h}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{2{k^2}}}{4} = \frac{{{k^2}}}{2}\left[ {\because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\sinh }}{h} = 1} \right]{/tex}

Also, {tex}f(0) = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{{{k^2}}}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow k = \pm 1{/tex}

question_text: Prove that the function f defined by {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{x}{{\left| x \right| + 2{x^2}}},\,if\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ k,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} remains discontinuous at x = 0, regardless the choice of k.
question_html: Prove that the function f defined by {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{x}{{\left| x \right| + 2{x^2}}},\,if\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ k,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}remains discontinuous at x = 0, regardless the choice of k.
solution_text: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{x}{{\left| x \right| + 2{x^2}}},\,if\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ k,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} At x = 0, {tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{x}{{\left| x \right| + 2{x^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {0 - h} \right)}}{{\left| {0 - h} \right| + 2{{\left( {0 - h} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0}} \frac{{ - h}}{{h + 2{h^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - h}}{{h\left( {1 + 2h} \right)}} = - 1{/tex} {tex}RHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0^ + }} \frac{x}{{\left| x \right| + 2{x^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{0 + h}}{{\left| {0 + h} \right| + 2{{\left( {0 + h} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0 }} \frac{h}{{h + 2{h^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{h}{{h\left( {1 + 2h} \right)}} = 1{/tex} And f(0) = k Since, LHL {tex}\ne {/tex} RHL for any value of k. Hence, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 0 regardless the choice of k.
solution_html:
{tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{x}{{\left| x \right| + 2{x^2}}},\,if\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ k,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}

At x = 0, {tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{x}{{\left| x \right| + 2{x^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {0 - h} \right)}}{{\left| {0 - h} \right| + 2{{\left( {0 - h} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0}} \frac{{ - h}}{{h + 2{h^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - h}}{{h\left( {1 + 2h} \right)}} = - 1{/tex}

{tex}RHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0^ + }} \frac{x}{{\left| x \right| + 2{x^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{0 + h}}{{\left| {0 + h} \right| + 2{{\left( {0 + h} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0 }} \frac{h}{{h + 2{h^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{h}{{h\left( {1 + 2h} \right)}} = 1{/tex}

And f(0) = k

Since, LHL {tex}\ne {/tex} RHL for any value of k.

Hence, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 0 regardless the choice of k.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the values of a and b such that the function f defined by {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + a,\,\,if\,\,x < 4 \hfill \\ a + b,\,\,if\,x = 4 \hfill \\ \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + b,\,\,if\,x > 4 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} is a continuous function at x = 4.
question_html:
Find the values of a and b such that the function f defined by

{tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + a,\,\,if\,\,x < 4 \hfill \\ a + b,\,\,if\,x = 4 \hfill \\ \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + b,\,\,if\,x > 4 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
is a continuous function at x = 4.
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + a,\,\,if\,\,x < 4 \hfill \\ a + b,\,\,if\,x = 4 \hfill \\ \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + b,\,\,if\,x > 4 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} At x = 4, {tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {4^ -}} \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + a{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0 }} \frac{{4 - h - 4}}{{\left| {4 - h - 4} \right|}} + a = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - h}}{h} + a{/tex} = -1 +a {tex}RHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {4^ + }} \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + b{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{4 + h - 4}}{{\left| {4 + h - 4} \right|}} + b = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{h}{h} + b = 1 + b{/tex} {tex}f(4) = a + b \Rightarrow - 1 + a = 1 + b = a + b{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - 1 + a = a + b{/tex} and {tex}1 + b = a + b{/tex} Therefore, we get, {tex} b = - 1{/tex} and a = 1
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + a,\,\,if\,\,x < 4 \hfill \\ a + b,\,\,if\,x = 4 \hfill \\ \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + b,\,\,if\,x > 4 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}

At x = 4, {tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {4^ -}} \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + a{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0 }} \frac{{4 - h - 4}}{{\left| {4 - h - 4} \right|}} + a = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - h}}{h} + a{/tex}

= -1 +a

{tex}RHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {4^ + }} \frac{{x - 4}}{{\left| {x - 4} \right|}} + b{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{4 + h - 4}}{{\left| {4 + h - 4} \right|}} + b = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{h}{h} + b = 1 + b{/tex}

{tex}f(4) = a + b \Rightarrow - 1 + a = 1 + b = a + b{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow - 1 + a = a + b{/tex} and {tex}1 + b = a + b{/tex}

Therefore, we get, {tex} b = - 1{/tex} and a = 1

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Given the function {tex}f(x) = \frac{1}{{x + 2}}{/tex} . Find the points of discontinuity of the composite function y = f(f(x)).
question_html: Given the function {tex}f(x) = \frac{1}{{x + 2}}{/tex}. Find the points of discontinuity of the composite function y = f(f(x)).
solution_text: We have {tex}f(x) = \frac{1}{{x + 2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore y = f\left\{ {f\left( x \right)} \right\}{/tex} {tex}= f\left( {\frac{1}{{x + 2}}} \right) = \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{{x + 2}} + 2}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + 2x + 4}}.\left( {x + 2} \right) = \frac{{\left( {x + 2} \right)}}{{\left( {2x + 5} \right)}}{/tex} So, the function y will not be continuous at those points, where it is not defined as it is a rational function. Therefore, {tex}y = \frac{{\left( {x + 2} \right)}}{{\left( {2x + 5} \right)}}{/tex} is not defined, when 2x + 5 = 0 {tex}\therefore x = \frac{{ - 5}}{2}{/tex} Hence, y is discontinuous at {tex}x = \frac{{ - 5}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have {tex}f(x) = \frac{1}{{x + 2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore y = f\left\{ {f\left( x \right)} \right\}{/tex} 
{tex}= f\left( {\frac{1}{{x + 2}}} \right) = \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{{x + 2}} + 2}}{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{{1 + 2x + 4}}.\left( {x + 2} \right) = \frac{{\left( {x + 2} \right)}}{{\left( {2x + 5} \right)}}{/tex} 
So, the function y will not be continuous at those points, where it is not defined as it is a rational function.
Therefore, {tex}y = \frac{{\left( {x + 2} \right)}}{{\left( {2x + 5} \right)}}{/tex} is not defined, when 2x + 5 = 0
{tex}\therefore x = \frac{{ - 5}}{2}{/tex} 
Hence, y is discontinuous at {tex}x = \frac{{ - 5}}{2}{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find all points of discontinuity of the function {tex}f(t) = \frac{1}{{{t^2} + t - 2}}{/tex} , where {tex}t = \frac{1}{{x - 1}}{/tex} .
question_html: Find all points of discontinuity of the function {tex}f(t) = \frac{1}{{{t^2} + t - 2}}{/tex}, where {tex}t = \frac{1}{{x - 1}}{/tex}.
solution_text: We have {tex}f(t) = \frac{1}{{{t^2} + t - 2}}{/tex} , and {tex}t = \frac{1}{{x - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore f(t) = \frac{1}{{\left( {\frac{1}{{{x^2} + 1 - 2x}}} \right) + \left( {\frac{1}{{x - 1}}} \right) - \frac{2}{1}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{\left( {\frac{{1 + x - 1\left[ { - 2{{\left( {x + 1} \right)}^2}} \right]}}{{\left( {{x^2} + 1 - 2x} \right)}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{x^2} + 1 - 2x}}{{x - 2{x^2} - 2 + 4x}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{x^2} + 1 - 2x}}{{ - 2{x^2} + 5x - 2}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{{{\left( {x - 1} \right)}^2}}}{{ - \left( {2{x^2} - 5x + 2} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{{\left( {x - 1} \right)}^2}}}{{\left( {2x - 1} \right)\left( {2 - x} \right)}}{/tex} So, {tex}f(t){/tex} is discontinuous at {tex}2x - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1/2{/tex} And {tex}2 - x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2{/tex}
solution_html:
We have {tex}f(t) = \frac{1}{{{t^2} + t - 2}}{/tex}, and {tex}t = \frac{1}{{x - 1}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore f(t) = \frac{1}{{\left( {\frac{1}{{{x^2} + 1 - 2x}}} \right) + \left( {\frac{1}{{x - 1}}} \right) - \frac{2}{1}}}{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{1}{{\left( {\frac{{1 + x - 1\left[ { - 2{{\left( {x + 1} \right)}^2}} \right]}}{{\left( {{x^2} + 1 - 2x} \right)}}} \right)}}{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{{{x^2} + 1 - 2x}}{{x - 2{x^2} - 2 + 4x}}{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{{{x^2} + 1 - 2x}}{{ - 2{x^2} + 5x - 2}}{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{{{{\left( {x - 1} \right)}^2}}}{{ - \left( {2{x^2} - 5x + 2} \right)}}{/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{{{{\left( {x - 1} \right)}^2}}}{{\left( {2x - 1} \right)\left( {2 - x} \right)}}{/tex} 
So, {tex}f(t){/tex} is discontinuous at {tex}2x - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1/2{/tex} 
And {tex}2 - x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Show that the function {tex}f(x) = \left| {\sin x + \cos x} \right|{/tex} is continuous at {tex}x = \pi .{/tex}
question_html: Show that the function {tex}f(x) = \left| {\sin x + \cos x} \right|{/tex} is continuous at {tex}x = \pi .{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}f(x) = \left| {\sin x + \cos x} \right|{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Let {tex}g(x) = \sin x + \cos x{/tex} And {tex}h(x) = \left| x \right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore hog(x) = h\left[ {g(x)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = h\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex} {tex} = \left| {\sin x + \cos x} \right|{/tex} Since {tex}g(x) = \sin x + \cos x{/tex} is a continuous function as it is formed by the addition of two continuous functions sin x and cos x. Also, {tex}h\left( x \right) = \left| x \right|{/tex} is also a continuous function. Since we know that the composite function of two continuous functions is also a continuous function. Hence, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {\sin x + \cos x} \right|{/tex} is a continuous function everywhere. So, f(x) is continuous at {tex}x = \pi {/tex}
solution_html:
We have {tex}f(x) = \left| {\sin x + \cos x} \right|{/tex} {tex}{/tex}
Let {tex}g(x) = \sin x + \cos x{/tex}
And {tex}h(x) = \left| x \right|{/tex}
{tex}\therefore hog(x) = h\left[ {g(x)} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = h\left( {\sin x + \cos x} \right){/tex}
{tex} = \left| {\sin x + \cos x} \right|{/tex}
Since {tex}g(x) = \sin x + \cos x{/tex} is a continuous function as it is formed by the addition of two continuous functions sin x and cos x.
Also, {tex}h\left( x \right) = \left| x \right|{/tex} is also a continuous function. Since we know that the composite function of two continuous functions is also a continuous function.
Hence, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {\sin x + \cos x} \right|{/tex} is a continuous function everywhere.
So, f(x) is continuous at {tex}x = \pi {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Examine the differentiability of f, where f is defined by {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x\left[ x \right],\,\,if\,0 \leqslant x < 2 \hfill \\ \left( {x - 1} \right)x,\,\,if\,2 \leqslant x < 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 2.
question_html:
Examine the differentiability of f, where f is defined by

{tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x\left[ x \right],\,\,if\,0 \leqslant x < 2 \hfill \\ \left( {x - 1} \right)x,\,\,if\,2 \leqslant x < 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 2.
solution_text: We have {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x\left[ x \right],\,\,if\,0 \leqslant x < 2 \hfill \\ \left( {x - 1} \right)x,\,\,if\,2 \leqslant x< 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} At x = 2, {tex}Lf'\left( 2 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f\left( {2 - h} \right) - f(2)}}{{ - h}}{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {2 - h} \right)\left[ {2 - h} \right] - \left( {2 - 1} \right)2}}{{ - h}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {2 - h} \right)\left( 1 \right) - 2}}{{ - h}}{/tex} {tex}[\therefore [a-h]=[a-1],where\ a\ is\ any\ positive\ integer]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{2 - h - 2}}{{ - h}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - h}}{{ - h}} = 1{/tex} {tex}Rf'\left( 2 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f\left( {2 + h} \right) - f\left( 2 \right)}}{h}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {2 + h - 1} \right)\left( {2 + h} \right) - \left( {2 - 1} \right).2}}{h}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {1 + h} \right)\left( {2 + h} \right) - 2}}{h}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{2 + h + 2h + {h^2} - 2}}{h}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{h^2} + 3h}}{h} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{h\left( {h + 3} \right)}}{h} = 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore Lf'\left( 2 \right) \ne Rf'\left( 2 \right){/tex} So, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 2.
solution_html:
We have {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x\left[ x \right],\,\,if\,0 \leqslant x < 2 \hfill \\ \left( {x - 1} \right)x,\,\,if\,2 \leqslant x< 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} 

At x = 2, {tex}Lf'\left( 2 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f\left( {2 - h} \right) - f(2)}}{{ - h}}{/tex}

{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {2 - h} \right)\left[ {2 - h} \right] - \left( {2 - 1} \right)2}}{{ - h}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {2 - h} \right)\left( 1 \right) - 2}}{{ - h}}{/tex} {tex}[\therefore [a-h]=[a-1],where\ a\ is\ any\ positive\ integer]{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{2 - h - 2}}{{ - h}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - h}}{{ - h}} = 1{/tex}

{tex}Rf'\left( 2 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f\left( {2 + h} \right) - f\left( 2 \right)}}{h}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {2 + h - 1} \right)\left( {2 + h} \right) - \left( {2 - 1} \right).2}}{h}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {1 + h} \right)\left( {2 + h} \right) - 2}}{h}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{2 + h + 2h + {h^2} - 2}}{h}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{h^2} + 3h}}{h} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{h\left( {h + 3} \right)}}{h} = 3{/tex}

{tex}\therefore Lf'\left( 2 \right) \ne Rf'\left( 2 \right){/tex}

So, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 2.

question_text: Examine the differentiability of f, where f is defined by {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} {x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 0.
question_html:
Examine the differentiability of f, where f is defined by

{tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} {x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 0.
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} {x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} For differentiability at x = 0, {tex}Lf'\left( 0 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{f\left( x \right) - f\left( 0 \right)}}{{x - 0}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{{x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x} - 0}}{{x - 0}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{{\left( {0 - h} \right)}^2}\sin \left( {\frac{1}{{0 - h}}} \right)}}{{0 - h}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{h^2}\sin \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{h}} \right)}}{{ - h}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} h\sin \left( {\frac{1}{h}} \right) ~~~ ...\left[ {\because \sin \left( { - \theta } \right) = - \sin \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= 0 \times{/tex} [an oscillating number between - 1 and 1] = 0 {tex}Rf'\left( 0 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{f(x) - f\left( 0 \right)}}{{x - 0}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{{x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x} - 0}}{{x - 0}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{{\left( {0 + h} \right)}^2}\sin \left( {\frac{1}{{0 + h}}} \right)}}{{0+h - 0}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0 }} \frac{{{h^2}\sin \left( {\frac{1}{h}} \right)}}{h}{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} h\sin \left( {\frac{1}{h}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 0 \times {/tex} [an oscillating number between - 1 and 1] = 0 {tex}\because Lf'\left( 0 \right) = Rf'\left( 0 \right){/tex} So, f(x) is differentiable at x = 0.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} {x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x},\,\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} 
For differentiability at x = 0,
{tex}Lf'\left( 0 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{f\left( x \right) - f\left( 0 \right)}}{{x - 0}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{{x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x} - 0}}{{x - 0}}{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{{\left( {0 - h} \right)}^2}\sin \left( {\frac{1}{{0 - h}}} \right)}}{{0 - h}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{h^2}\sin \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{h}} \right)}}{{ - h}}{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} h\sin \left( {\frac{1}{h}} \right) ~~~ ...\left[ {\because \sin \left( { - \theta } \right) = - \sin \theta } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= 0 \times{/tex}[an oscillating number between - 1 and 1] = 0
{tex}Rf'\left( 0 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{f(x) - f\left( 0 \right)}}{{x - 0}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{{x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x} - 0}}{{x - 0}}{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{{{\left( {0 + h} \right)}^2}\sin \left( {\frac{1}{{0 + h}}} \right)}}{{0+h - 0}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0 }} \frac{{{h^2}\sin \left( {\frac{1}{h}} \right)}}{h}{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} h\sin \left( {\frac{1}{h}} \right){/tex}
{tex} = 0 \times {/tex} [an oscillating number between - 1 and 1] = 0
{tex}\because Lf'\left( 0 \right) = Rf'\left( 0 \right){/tex}
So, f(x) is differentiable at x = 0.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Examine the differentiability of f, where f is defined by {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 1 + x,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant 2 \hfill \\ 5 - x,\,\,if\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 2.
question_html: Examine the differentiability of f, where f is defined by {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 1 + x,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant 2 \hfill \\ 5 - x,\,\,if\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 2.
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 1 + x,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant 2 \hfill \\ 5 - x,\,\,if\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} For differentiability at x = 2. {tex}Lf'\left( 2 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} \frac{{f(x) - f(2)}}{{x - 2}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} \frac{{\left( {1 + x} \right) - \left( {1 + 2} \right)}}{{x - 2}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {1 + 2 - h} \right)-3}}{{2 - h - 2}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - h}}{{ - h}} = 1{/tex} {tex}Rf'\left( 2 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \frac{{f(x) - f(2)}}{{x - 2}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \frac{{\left( {5 - x} \right) - 3}}{{x - 2}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{5 - \left( {2 + h} \right) - 3}}{{2 + h - 2}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{5 - 2 - h - 3}}{h} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - h}}{{ h}}{/tex} = - 1 {tex}\because Lf'\left( 2 \right) \ne Rf'\left( 2 \right){/tex} So, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 2.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 1 + x,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant 2 \hfill \\ 5 - x,\,\,if\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} 

For differentiability at x = 2.

{tex}Lf'\left( 2 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} \frac{{f(x) - f(2)}}{{x - 2}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} \frac{{\left( {1 + x} \right) - \left( {1 + 2} \right)}}{{x - 2}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left( {1 + 2 - h} \right)-3}}{{2 - h - 2}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - h}}{{ - h}} = 1{/tex}

{tex}Rf'\left( 2 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \frac{{f(x) - f(2)}}{{x - 2}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \frac{{\left( {5 - x} \right) - 3}}{{x - 2}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{5 - \left( {2 + h} \right) - 3}}{{2 + h - 2}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{5 - 2 - h - 3}}{h} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - h}}{{ h}}{/tex} = - 1

{tex}\because Lf'\left( 2 \right) \ne Rf'\left( 2 \right){/tex}

So, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 2.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Show that {tex}f(x) = \left| {x - 5} \right|{/tex} is continuous but not differentiable at x = 5.
question_html: Show that {tex}f(x) = \left| {x - 5} \right|{/tex} is continuous but not differentiable at x = 5.
solution_text: We have {tex}f(x) = \left| {x - 5} \right|{/tex} {tex}\therefore f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} - \left( {x - 5} \right),\,\,if\,\,x < 5 \hfill \\ x - 5,\,\,if\,x \geqslant 5\, \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} For continuity at x = 5, {tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \left( { - x + 5} \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ { - \left( {5 - h} \right) + 5} \right] = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} h = 0{/tex} {tex}RHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ + }} \left( {x - 5} \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left( {5 + h - 5} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} h = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore f(5) = 5 - 5 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow LHL = RHL = f(5){/tex} Hence, f(x) is continuous at x = 5. Now, {tex}Lf'\left( 5 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \frac{{f\left( x \right) - f\left( 5 \right)}}{{x - 5}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \frac{{ - x + 5 - 0}}{{x - 5}} = - 1{/tex} {tex}Rf'\left( 5 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \frac{{f\left( x \right) - f\left( 5 \right)}}{{x - 5}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \frac{{x - 5 - 0}}{{x - 5}} = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore Lf'\left( 5 \right) \ne Rf'\left( 5 \right){/tex} So, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {x - 5} \right|{/tex} is not differentiable at x = 5.
solution_html:
We have {tex}f(x) = \left| {x - 5} \right|{/tex}

{tex}\therefore f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} - \left( {x - 5} \right),\,\,if\,\,x < 5 \hfill \\ x - 5,\,\,if\,x \geqslant 5\, \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}

For continuity at x = 5,

{tex}LHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \left( { - x + 5} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ { - \left( {5 - h} \right) + 5} \right] = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} h = 0{/tex}

{tex}RHL = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ + }} \left( {x - 5} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left( {5 + h - 5} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} h = 0{/tex}

{tex}\therefore f(5) = 5 - 5 = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow LHL = RHL = f(5){/tex}

Hence, f(x) is continuous at x = 5.

Now, {tex}Lf'\left( 5 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \frac{{f\left( x \right) - f\left( 5 \right)}}{{x - 5}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \frac{{ - x + 5 - 0}}{{x - 5}} = - 1{/tex}

{tex}Rf'\left( 5 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \frac{{f\left( x \right) - f\left( 5 \right)}}{{x - 5}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} \frac{{x - 5 - 0}}{{x - 5}} = 1{/tex}

{tex}\therefore Lf'\left( 5 \right) \ne Rf'\left( 5 \right){/tex}

So, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {x - 5} \right|{/tex} is not differentiable at x = 5.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: A function {tex}f:R \to R{/tex} satisfies the equation {tex}f\left( {x + y} \right) = f\left( x \right).f\left( y \right){/tex} for all {tex}x,y \in R,f\left( x \right) \ne 0{/tex} Suppose that the function is differentiable at {tex}x = 0{/tex} and {tex}f'\left( 0 \right) = 2{/tex} , then prove that {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 2f\left( x \right){/tex}
question_html: A function {tex}f:R \to R{/tex} satisfies the equation {tex}f\left( {x + y} \right) = f\left( x \right).f\left( y \right){/tex}for all {tex}x,y \in R,f\left( x \right) \ne 0{/tex} Suppose that the function is differentiable at {tex}x = 0{/tex} and {tex}f'\left( 0 \right) = 2{/tex}, then prove that {tex}f'\left( x \right) = 2f\left( x \right){/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}f:R \to R{/tex} satisfies the equation {tex}f\left( {x + y} \right) = f\left( x \right).f\left( y \right){/tex} , {tex}\forall x,y \in R,f(x) \ne 0{/tex} Let f(x) is differentiable at x = 0 and f '(0) = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( 0 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h\to 0} \frac{{f(0+h) - f(0)}}{{0+h - 0}}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} since f(x+y)=f(x)f(y), therefore f(0+h)=f(0)f(h) and f'(0)=2, therefore,we get {tex} 2 = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(0).f\left( h \right) - f\left( 0 \right)}}{h}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(0)\left[ {f\left( h \right) - 1} \right]}}{h}{/tex} .....(1) Also, {tex}f'\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f\left( {x + h} \right) - f\left( x \right)}}{h}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f\left( x \right).f\left( h \right) - f\left( x \right)}}{h}\left[ {\because f\left( {x + y} \right) = f\left( x \right).f\left( y \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f\left( x \right)\left[ {f\left( h \right) - 1} \right]}}{h}{/tex} {tex}=f(x). \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left[ {f\left( h \right) - 1} \right]}}{h}{/tex} = 2f(x) [using (1)] {tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) = 2f\left( x \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}f:R \to R{/tex} satisfies the equation {tex}f\left( {x + y} \right) = f\left( x \right).f\left( y \right){/tex}, {tex}\forall x,y \in R,f(x) \ne 0{/tex}

Let f(x) is differentiable at x = 0 and f '(0) = 2

{tex}\Rightarrow f'\left( 0 \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h\to 0} \frac{{f(0+h) - f(0)}}{{0+h - 0}}{/tex}

{tex}{/tex}since f(x+y)=f(x)f(y), therefore f(0+h)=f(0)f(h) and f'(0)=2, therefore,we get

{tex} 2 = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(0).f\left( h \right) - f\left( 0 \right)}}{h}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 2 = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(0)\left[ {f\left( h \right) - 1} \right]}}{h}{/tex}.....(1)

Also, {tex}f'\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f\left( {x + h} \right) - f\left( x \right)}}{h}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f\left( x \right).f\left( h \right) - f\left( x \right)}}{h}\left[ {\because f\left( {x + y} \right) = f\left( x \right).f\left( y \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f\left( x \right)\left[ {f\left( h \right) - 1} \right]}}{h}{/tex}

{tex}=f(x). \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\left[ {f\left( h \right) - 1} \right]}}{h}{/tex}

= 2f(x) [using (1)]

{tex}\therefore f'\left( x \right) = 2f\left( x \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}2^{\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} .
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}2^{\cos ^{2} x}{/tex}.
solution_text: Let y = {tex}2^{\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} (taking log on both sides) {tex}\therefore{/tex} log y = log 2 {tex}log2^{Cos^2x}{/tex} = cos 2 x.log 2 On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d}{d y} \log y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \log 2 \cdot \cos ^{2} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\log 2 \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\log 2 \cdot[2 \cos x] \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \cos x{/tex} = log 2.2 cos x.(-sin x) = log 2.[-(sin 2x)] {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=-y \cdot \log 2(\sin 2 x){/tex} {tex}=-2^{\cos ^{2} x} \cdot \log 2(\sin 2 x){/tex}
solution_html:
Let y = {tex}2^{\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} (taking log on both sides)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} log y = log 2{tex}log2^{Cos^2x}{/tex}= cos2x.log 2
On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d}{d y} \log y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \log 2 \cdot \cos ^{2} x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\log 2 \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\log 2 \cdot[2 \cos x] \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \cos x{/tex}
= log 2.2 cos x.(-sin x)
= log 2.[-(sin 2x)]
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=-y \cdot \log 2(\sin 2 x){/tex}
{tex}=-2^{\cos ^{2} x} \cdot \log 2(\sin 2 x){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\frac{8^{x}}{x^{8}}{/tex}
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\frac{8^{x}}{x^{8}}{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}y=\frac{8^{x}}{x^{8}} \Rightarrow \log y=\log \frac{8^{x}}{x^{8}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d y} \log y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\log 8^{x}-\log x^{8}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=[x \cdot \log 8-8 . \log x]{/tex} on differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\log 8.1-8 \cdot \frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\log 8-\frac{8}{x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=y\left(\log 8-\frac{8}{x}\right)=\frac{8^{x}}{x^{8}}\left(\log 8-\frac{8}{x}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\frac{8^{x}}{x^{8}} \Rightarrow \log y=\log \frac{8^{x}}{x^{8}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d y} \log y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\log 8^{x}-\log x^{8}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=[x \cdot \log 8-8 . \log x]{/tex}
on differentiating w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\log 8.1-8 \cdot \frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\log 8-\frac{8}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=y\left(\log 8-\frac{8}{x}\right)=\frac{8^{x}}{x^{8}}\left(\log 8-\frac{8}{x}\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\log (x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a}) {/tex}
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\log (x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a}) {/tex}
solution_text: Let y = {tex}\log (x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a}){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \log (x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a})} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}[x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a}]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a})}\left[1+\frac{1}{2}\left(x^{2}+a\right)^{-12} \cdot 2 x\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a})} \cdot\left(1+\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{(\sqrt{x^{2}+a}+x)}{(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a})(\sqrt{x^{2}+a})}=\frac{1}{(\sqrt{x^{2}+a})}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let y = {tex}\log (x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \log (x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a})} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}[x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a}]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a})}\left[1+\frac{1}{2}\left(x^{2}+a\right)^{-12} \cdot 2 x\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a})} \cdot\left(1+\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(\sqrt{x^{2}+a}+x)}{(x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a})(\sqrt{x^{2}+a})}=\frac{1}{(\sqrt{x^{2}+a})}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\log \left[\log \left(\log x^{5}\right)\right]{/tex}
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\log \left[\log \left(\log x^{5}\right)\right]{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}y=\log \left[\log \left(\log x^{5}\right)\right]{/tex} differentiating w.r.t. {tex}x{/tex} on both sides, we get {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\log \left(\log \log x^{5}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\log \log x^{5}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(\log \cdot \log x^{5}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\log \log x^{5}} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{\log x^{5}}\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \log x^{5}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\log \log x^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{\log x^{5}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(5 \log x){/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{x \cdot \log \left(\log x^{5}\right) \cdot \log \left(x^{5}\right)}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\log \left[\log \left(\log x^{5}\right)\right]{/tex}
differentiating w.r.t. {tex}x{/tex} on both sides, we get
{tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\log \left(\log \log x^{5}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\log \log x^{5}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(\log \cdot \log x^{5}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\log \log x^{5}} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{\log x^{5}}\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \log x^{5}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\log \log x^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{\log x^{5}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(5 \log x){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5}{x \cdot \log \left(\log x^{5}\right) \cdot \log \left(x^{5}\right)}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\sin \sqrt{x}+\cos ^{2} \sqrt{x}{/tex}
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\sin \sqrt{x}+\cos ^{2} \sqrt{x}{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}y=\sin \sqrt{x}+(\cos \sqrt{x})^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \sin \left(x^{1 / 2}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left[\cos \left(x^{1 / 2}\right)\right]^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\cos x^{1/2} \frac{d}{d x} x^{1/2}+2 \cos \left(x^{1/2}\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left[\cos \left(x^{1 / 2}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\cos \left(x^{1/2}\right) \frac{1}{2} x^{-1/2}+2 \cdot \cos \left(x^{1/2}\right) \cdot\left[-\sin \left(x^{1/2}\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x} x^{1/2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\cos \sqrt{x} \cdot \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}\left[-2 \cos \left(x^{1 / 2}\right)\right] \cdot \sin x^{1 / 2} \cdot \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}[\cos (\sqrt{x})-\sin (2 \sqrt{x})]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\sin \sqrt{x}+(\cos \sqrt{x})^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \sin \left(x^{1 / 2}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left[\cos \left(x^{1 / 2}\right)\right]^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\cos x^{1/2} \frac{d}{d x} x^{1/2}+2 \cos \left(x^{1/2}\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left[\cos \left(x^{1 / 2}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\cos \left(x^{1/2}\right) \frac{1}{2} x^{-1/2}+2 \cdot \cos \left(x^{1/2}\right) \cdot\left[-\sin \left(x^{1/2}\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x} x^{1/2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\cos \sqrt{x} \cdot \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}\left[-2 \cos \left(x^{1 / 2}\right)\right] \cdot \sin x^{1 / 2} \cdot \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}[\cos (\sqrt{x})-\sin (2 \sqrt{x})]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: sin n (ax 2 + bx + c)
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: sinn (ax2 + bx + c)
solution_text: Let sin n (ax 2 + bx + c) {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\sin \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right)\right]^{n}{/tex} {tex}=n\left[\sin \left(\alpha x^{2}+b x+c\right)\right]^{n-1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \sin \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right){/tex} {tex}=n \cdot \sin ^{n-1}\left(\alpha x^{2}+b x+c\right) \cdot \cos \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right){/tex} {tex}=n \cdot \sin ^{n-1}\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) \cdot \cos \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) \cdot(2 a x+b){/tex} {tex}=n \cdot(2 a x+b) \cdot \sin ^{n-1}\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) \cdot \cos \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let sinn(ax2 + bx + c)
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\sin \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right)\right]^{n}{/tex}
{tex}=n\left[\sin \left(\alpha x^{2}+b x+c\right)\right]^{n-1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \sin \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right){/tex}
{tex}=n \cdot \sin ^{n-1}\left(\alpha x^{2}+b x+c\right) \cdot \cos \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right){/tex}
{tex}=n \cdot \sin ^{n-1}\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) \cdot \cos \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) \cdot(2 a x+b){/tex}
{tex}=n \cdot(2 a x+b) \cdot \sin ^{n-1}\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right) \cdot \cos \left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\cos (\tan \sqrt{x+1}){/tex}
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\cos (\tan \sqrt{x+1}){/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}y=\cos (\tan \sqrt{x+1}){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \cos (\tan \sqrt{x+1}){/tex} {tex}=-\sin (\tan \sqrt{x+1}) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\tan \sqrt{x+1}){/tex} {tex}=-\sin (\tan \sqrt{x+1}) \cdot \sec ^{2} \sqrt{x+1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+1)^{1 / 2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{d}{d x}(\tan x)=\sec ^{2} x\right]{/tex} {tex}=-\sin (\tan \sqrt{x+1}) \cdot(\sec \sqrt{x+1})^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}(x+1)^{-1 / 2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+1){/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}} \cdot \sin (\tan \sqrt{x+1}) \cdot \sec ^{2}(\sqrt{x+1}){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\cos (\tan \sqrt{x+1}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \cos (\tan \sqrt{x+1}){/tex}
{tex}=-\sin (\tan \sqrt{x+1}) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\tan \sqrt{x+1}){/tex}
{tex}=-\sin (\tan \sqrt{x+1}) \cdot \sec ^{2} \sqrt{x+1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+1)^{1 / 2}{/tex}      {tex}\left[\because \frac{d}{d x}(\tan x)=\sec ^{2} x\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-\sin (\tan \sqrt{x+1}) \cdot(\sec \sqrt{x+1})^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}(x+1)^{-1 / 2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+1){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}} \cdot \sin (\tan \sqrt{x+1}) \cdot \sec ^{2}(\sqrt{x+1}){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: sinx 2 + sin 2 x + sin 2 (x 2 )
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: sinx2 + sin2x + sin2(x2)
solution_text: Let y = sinx 2 + sin 2 x + sin 2 (x 2 ) {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \sin \left(x^{2}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)^{2}+\frac{d}{d x}\left(\sin x^{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\cos \left(x^{2}\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}\right)+2 \sin x \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \sin x+2 \sin x^{2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \sin x^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\cos x^{2} 2 x+2 \cdot \sin x \cdot \cos x+2 \sin x^{2} \cos x^{2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}=2 x \cos (x)^{2}+2 \cdot \sin x \cdot \cos x+2 \sin x^{2} \cdot \cos x^{2} \cdot 2 x{/tex} {tex}=2 x \cos (x)^{2}+\sin 2 x+\sin 2(x)^{2} \cdot 2 x{/tex} {tex}=2 x \cos (x)^{2}+2 x \cdot \sin 2\left(x^{2}\right)+\sin 2 x{/tex}
solution_html:
Let y = sinx2 + sin2x + sin2(x2)
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \sin \left(x^{2}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)^{2}+\frac{d}{d x}\left(\sin x^{2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\cos \left(x^{2}\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}\right)+2 \sin x \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \sin x+2 \sin x^{2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \sin x^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\cos x^{2} 2 x+2 \cdot \sin x \cdot \cos x+2 \sin x^{2} \cos x^{2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} x^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=2 x \cos (x)^{2}+2 \cdot \sin x \cdot \cos x+2 \sin x^{2} \cdot \cos x^{2} \cdot 2 x{/tex}
{tex}=2 x \cos (x)^{2}+\sin 2 x+\sin 2(x)^{2} \cdot 2 x{/tex}
{tex}=2 x \cos (x)^{2}+2 x \cdot \sin 2\left(x^{2}\right)+\sin 2 x{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+1}}\right){/tex}
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+1}}\right){/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+1}}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+1}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+1}}\right)^{2}}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \frac{1}{(x+1)^{12}}\left[\because \frac{d}{d x}\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{x+1-1}{x+1}}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \cdot(x+1)^{-12}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{x+1}{x}} \cdot \frac{-1}{2}(x+1)^{-\frac{1}{2}-1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+1){/tex} {tex}=\frac{(x+1)^{1 / 2}}{x^{12}} \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)(x+1)^{-3 / 2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{x}} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x+1}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+1}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+1}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+1}}\right)^{2}}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \frac{1}{(x+1)^{12}}\left[\because \frac{d}{d x}\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{x+1-1}{x+1}}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \cdot(x+1)^{-12}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{x+1}{x}} \cdot \frac{-1}{2}(x+1)^{-\frac{1}{2}-1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+1){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(x+1)^{1 / 2}}{x^{12}} \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)(x+1)^{-3 / 2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{x}} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x+1}\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t x: (sin x) cos x
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t x: (sin x)cos x
solution_text: Let y = (sin x) cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = log(sin x) cosx = cosx logsin x {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{d y} \log y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}(\cos x \cdot \log \sin x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos x \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \log \sin x+\log \sin x \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \cos x{/tex} {tex}=\cos x \cdot \frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \sin x+\log \sin x \cdot(-\sin x){/tex} {tex}=\cot x \cos x-\log (\sin x) \cdot \sin x~~~~~...\left [\because \cot x=\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=y\left(\frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\sin x}-\sin x \log (\sin x)\right){/tex} {tex}=\sin x^{\cos x}\left(\frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\sin x}-\sin x \cdot \log (\sin x)\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let y = (sin x)cos x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = log(sin x)cosx = cosx logsin x
{tex}\therefore \frac{d}{d y} \log y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}(\cos x \cdot \log \sin x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos x \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \log \sin x+\log \sin x \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \cos x{/tex}
{tex}=\cos x \cdot \frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \sin x+\log \sin x \cdot(-\sin x){/tex}
{tex}=\cot x \cos x-\log (\sin x) \cdot \sin x~~~~~...\left [\because \cot x=\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=y\left(\frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\sin x}-\sin x \log (\sin x)\right){/tex}
{tex}=\sin x^{\cos x}\left(\frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\sin x}-\sin x \cdot \log (\sin x)\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\sin ^{m} x \cdot \cos ^{n} x{/tex}
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\sin ^{m} x \cdot \cos ^{n} x{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}y=\sin ^{m} x \cdot \cos ^{n} x{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[(\sin x)^{m} \cdot(\cos x)^{n}\right]{/tex} {tex}=(\sin x)^{m} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)^{n}+(\cos x)^{n} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)^{n}{/tex} {tex}=(\sin x)^{m} \cdot n(\cos x)^{n-1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \cos x+(\cos x)^{n} m(\sin x)^{m-1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \sin x{/tex} {tex}=(\sin x)^{m} \cdot n(\cos x)^{n-1}(-\sin x)+(\cos x)^{n} \cdot m(\sin x)^{m-1} \cos x{/tex} {tex}=-n \sin ^{m} x \cos ^{n-1} x \cdot(\sin x)+m \cos ^{n} x \cdot \sin ^{n-1} x \cdot \cos x{/tex} {tex}=-n \sin ^{m} x \sin x \cdot \cos ^{n} x \frac{1}{\cos x}+m \cdot \sin ^{m} x \cdot \frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \cos ^{n} x \cdot \cos x{/tex} {tex}=-n \sin ^{m} x \cos ^{n} x \cdot \tan x+m \sin ^{\pi} x \cdot \cos ^{n} x \cdot \cot x{/tex} {tex}=\sin ^{m} x \cdot \cos ^{n} x(-n \tan x+m \cot x){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\sin ^{m} x \cdot \cos ^{n} x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[(\sin x)^{m} \cdot(\cos x)^{n}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=(\sin x)^{m} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)^{n}+(\cos x)^{n} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)^{n}{/tex}
{tex}=(\sin x)^{m} \cdot n(\cos x)^{n-1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \cos x+(\cos x)^{n} m(\sin x)^{m-1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} \sin x{/tex}
{tex}=(\sin x)^{m} \cdot n(\cos x)^{n-1}(-\sin x)+(\cos x)^{n} \cdot m(\sin x)^{m-1} \cos x{/tex}
{tex}=-n \sin ^{m} x \cos ^{n-1} x \cdot(\sin x)+m \cos ^{n} x \cdot \sin ^{n-1} x \cdot \cos x{/tex}
{tex}=-n \sin ^{m} x \sin x \cdot \cos ^{n} x \frac{1}{\cos x}+m \cdot \sin ^{m} x \cdot \frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \cos ^{n} x \cdot \cos x{/tex}
{tex}=-n \sin ^{m} x \cos ^{n} x \cdot \tan x+m \sin ^{\pi} x \cdot \cos ^{n} x \cdot \cot x{/tex}
{tex}=\sin ^{m} x \cdot \cos ^{n} x(-n \tan x+m \cot x){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: (x + 1) 2 + (x + 2) 3 .(x + 3) 4
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: (x + 1)2 + (x + 2)3.(x + 3)4
solution_text: Let y = (x + 1) 2 + (x + 2) 3 (x + 3) 4 Taking log on both sides, we get {tex}\therefore \log y=\log \left\{(x+1)^{2} \cdot(x+2)^{3}(x+3)^{4}\right\}{/tex} = log(x + 1) 2 + log(x + 2) 3 + log(x + 3) 4 and {tex}\frac{d}{d y} \log y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}[2 \log (x+1)]{/tex} {tex}+\frac{d}{d x}[3 \log (x+2)]+\frac{d}{d x}[4 \log (x+3)]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2}{(x+1)} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+1)+3 \cdot \frac{1}{(x+2)} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+2){/tex} {tex}+4 \cdot \frac{1}{(x+3)} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+3)~~~...\left[\because \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)=\frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{2}{x+1}+\frac{3}{x+2}+\frac{4}{x+3}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=y\left[\frac{2}{x+1}+\frac{3}{x+2}+\frac{4}{x+3}\right]{/tex} {tex}=(x+1)^{2} \cdot(x+2)^{3} \cdot(x+3)^{4}\left[\frac{2}{x+1}+\frac{3}{x+2}+\frac{4}{x+3}\right]{/tex} {tex}=(x+1)^{2} \cdot(x+2)^{3} \cdot(x+3)^{4}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{2(x+2)(x+3)+3(x+1)(x+3)+4(x+1)(x+2)}{(x+1)(x+2)(x+3)}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(x+1)^{2}(x+2)^{3}(x+3)^{4}}{(x+1)(x+2)(x+3)}{/tex} [2(x 2 + 5x + 6) + 3(x 2 + 4x + 3) + 4(x 2 + 3x + 2)] = (x + 1)(x + 2) 2 (x + 3) 3 [2x 2 + 10x + 12 + 3x 2 + 12x + 9 + 4x 2 + 12x + 8] = (x + 1)(x + 2) 2 (x + 3) 3 ( 9x 2 + 34x + 29)
solution_html:
Let y = (x + 1)2 + (x + 2)3(x + 3)4
Taking log on both sides, we get
{tex}\therefore \log y=\log \left\{(x+1)^{2} \cdot(x+2)^{3}(x+3)^{4}\right\}{/tex}
= log(x + 1)2 + log(x + 2)3 + log(x + 3)4
and {tex}\frac{d}{d y} \log y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}[2 \log (x+1)]{/tex}{tex}+\frac{d}{d x}[3 \log (x+2)]+\frac{d}{d x}[4 \log (x+3)]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2}{(x+1)} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+1)+3 \cdot \frac{1}{(x+2)} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+2){/tex}
{tex}+4 \cdot \frac{1}{(x+3)} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x+3)~~~...\left[\because \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)=\frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\frac{2}{x+1}+\frac{3}{x+2}+\frac{4}{x+3}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=y\left[\frac{2}{x+1}+\frac{3}{x+2}+\frac{4}{x+3}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=(x+1)^{2} \cdot(x+2)^{3} \cdot(x+3)^{4}\left[\frac{2}{x+1}+\frac{3}{x+2}+\frac{4}{x+3}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=(x+1)^{2} \cdot(x+2)^{3} \cdot(x+3)^{4}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\frac{2(x+2)(x+3)+3(x+1)(x+3)+4(x+1)(x+2)}{(x+1)(x+2)(x+3)}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(x+1)^{2}(x+2)^{3}(x+3)^{4}}{(x+1)(x+2)(x+3)}{/tex}
[2(x2 + 5x + 6) + 3(x2 + 4x + 3) + 4(x2 + 3x + 2)]
= (x + 1)(x + 2)2(x + 3)3
[2x2 + 10x + 12 + 3x2 + 12x + 9 + 4x2 + 12x + 8]
= (x + 1)(x + 2)2(x + 3)3 (9x2 + 34x + 29)

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sqrt{2}}\right) ; ~where~\frac{-\pi}{4}<x<\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sqrt{2}}\right) ; ~where~\frac{-\pi}{4}<x<\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
