question_text: Verify Rolle’s theorem for the function: f(x) = x(x + 3)e -x/2 in [-3, 0]
question_html: Verify Rolle’s theorem for the function: f(x) = x(x + 3)e-x/2 in [-3, 0]
solution_text: We have, f(x) = x(x + 3)e -x/2 f(x) s a continuous function. [since, it is a combination of polynomial functions x(x + 3) and an exponential function e -x/2 which are continuous functions] So, f(x) = x(x + 3)e -x/2 is continuous in [-3, 0] {tex}\therefore f^{\prime}(x)=\left(x^{2}+3 x\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x} e^{-x / 2}+e^{-x 2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}+3 x\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(x^{2}+3 x\right) \cdot e^{-x / 2} \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+e^{-x / 2} \cdot(2 x+3){/tex} {tex}=e^{-x / 2}\left[2 x+3-\frac{1}{2} \cdot\left(x^{2}+3 x\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=e^{-x / 2}\left[\frac{4 x+6-x^{2}-3 x}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}=e^{-x / 2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[-x^{2}+x+6\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{2} e^{-x 2}\left[x^{2}-x-6\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{2} e^{-x / 2}\left[x^{2}-3 x+2 x-6\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-1}{2} e^{-x / 2}[(x+2)(x-3)]{/tex} which exists in (-3, 0) Hence, f(x) is differentiable in (-3, 0) {tex}\therefore f(-3)=-3(-3+3) e^{-32}=0{/tex} and f(0) = 0(0 + 3)e -0/2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(-3) = f(0) Since, condtions of Rolle's theorem are satisfied. Hence, there exists a real number c such that f'(c) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{1}{2} e^{-c / 2}(c+2)(c-3)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -2, 3, wehre -2 {tex}\in{/tex} (-3, 0) Therefore, Rolle's theorem has been verified.
solution_html:
We have, f(x) = x(x + 3)e-x/2

  1. f(x) s a continuous function. [since, it is a combination of polynomial functions x(x + 3) and an exponential function e-x/2 which are continuous functions]
    So, f(x) = x(x + 3)e-x/2 is continuous in [-3, 0]
  2. {tex}\therefore f^{\prime}(x)=\left(x^{2}+3 x\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x} e^{-x / 2}+e^{-x 2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}+3 x\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\left(x^{2}+3 x\right) \cdot e^{-x / 2} \cdot\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+e^{-x / 2} \cdot(2 x+3){/tex}
    {tex}=e^{-x / 2}\left[2 x+3-\frac{1}{2} \cdot\left(x^{2}+3 x\right)\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=e^{-x / 2}\left[\frac{4 x+6-x^{2}-3 x}{2}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=e^{-x / 2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[-x^{2}+x+6\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{-1}{2} e^{-x 2}\left[x^{2}-x-6\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{-1}{2} e^{-x / 2}\left[x^{2}-3 x+2 x-6\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{-1}{2} e^{-x / 2}[(x+2)(x-3)]{/tex} which exists in (-3, 0)
    Hence, f(x) is differentiable in (-3, 0)
  3. {tex}\therefore f(-3)=-3(-3+3) e^{-32}=0{/tex}
    and f(0) = 0(0 + 3)e-0/2 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(-3) = f(0)
    Since, condtions of Rolle's theorem are satisfied.
    Hence, there exists a real number c such that f'(c) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{1}{2} e^{-c / 2}(c+2)(c-3)=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = -2, 3, wehre -2 {tex}\in{/tex}(-3, 0)
    Therefore, Rolle's theorem has been verified.

question_text: Using Rolle's theorem for the function f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{4-x^{2}}{/tex} on [-2, 2].
question_html: Using Rolle's theorem for the function f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{4-x^{2}}{/tex} on [-2, 2].
solution_text: Clearly, f(x) is defined for all x {tex}\in{/tex} [- 2, 2] and has a unique value for each x {tex}\in{/tex} [-2, 2]. So, at each point of [-2,2], the limit off(x) is equal to the value of the function. Therefore, f(x) is continuous on [-2,2]. Now, f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{4-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}\frac{-x}{\sqrt{4-x^{2}}}{/tex} Clearly, f'(x) = {tex}\frac{-x}{\sqrt{4-x^{2}}}{/tex} exists for all x {tex}\in{/tex} (- 2, 2). So, f(x) is differentiable on (-2,2). Also, f(-2) = (2) = 0. Thus, all the three conditions of Rolle's theorem are satisfied. Now we have to show that there exists c {tex}\in{/tex} (- 2, 2) such that f'(c) = 0. We have, f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{4-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}\frac{-x}{\sqrt{4-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-x}{\sqrt{4-x^{2}}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 Since c = 0 {tex}\in{/tex} (- 2, 2) such that f'(c) = 0. Hence, Rolle's theorem is verified.
solution_html:
Clearly, f(x) is defined for all x {tex}\in{/tex} [- 2, 2] and has a unique value for each x {tex}\in{/tex} [-2, 2]. So, at each point of [-2,2], the limit off(x) is equal to the value of the function. Therefore, f(x) is continuous on [-2,2].
Now, f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{4-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}\frac{-x}{\sqrt{4-x^{2}}}{/tex}
Clearly, f'(x) = {tex}\frac{-x}{\sqrt{4-x^{2}}}{/tex} exists for all x {tex}\in{/tex} (- 2, 2). So, f(x) is differentiable on (-2,2).
Also, f(-2) = (2) = 0. Thus, all the three conditions of Rolle's theorem are satisfied. Now we have to show that there exists c {tex}\in{/tex} (- 2, 2) such that f'(c) = 0.
We have,
f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{4-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = {tex}\frac{-x}{\sqrt{4-x^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{-x}{\sqrt{4-x^{2}}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0 
Since c = 0 {tex}\in{/tex} (- 2, 2) such that f'(c) = 0. Hence, Rolle's theorem is verified.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Discuss the applicability of Rolle’s theorem on the function given by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {x^{2}+1, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {3-x, \text { if } 1 \leq x \leq 2} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_html: Discuss the applicability of Rolle’s theorem on the function given by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {x^{2}+1, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {3-x, \text { if } 1 \leq x \leq 2} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {x^{2}+1, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {3-x, \text { if } 1 \leq x \leq 2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} We know that polynomial function is everywhere continuous and differentiability. So, f(x) is continuous and differentiable at all points except possibly at x = 1. Now, check the differentiability at x = 1 At x = 1, Left hand derivative {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} \frac{{f(x) - f(1)}}{{x - 1}}{/tex} {tex}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{\left(x^{2}+1\right)-(1+1)}{x-1}\left[\because f(x)=x^{2}+1, \forall 0 \leq x \leq 1\right]{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{x^{2}-1}{x-1}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(x+1)(x-1)}{x-1}{/tex} = 2 and Right hand derivative {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(3-x) f(1+1)}{(x-1)}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{3-x-2}{x-1}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{-(x-1)}{x-1}=-1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Left hand derivative {tex} \neq {/tex} Right hand derivative So, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 1. Hence, Rolle’s theorem is not applicable on the interval [0, 2].
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {x^{2}+1, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {3-x, \text { if } 1 \leq x \leq 2} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
We know that polynomial function is everywhere continuous and differentiability.
So, f(x) is continuous and differentiable at all points except possibly at x = 1.
Now, check the differentiability at x = 1
At x = 1,
Left hand derivative {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} \frac{{f(x) - f(1)}}{{x - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{\left(x^{2}+1\right)-(1+1)}{x-1}\left[\because f(x)=x^{2}+1, \forall 0 \leq x \leq 1\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{x^{2}-1}{x-1}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(x+1)(x-1)}{x-1}{/tex}
= 2
and Right hand derivative {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{(3-x) f(1+1)}{(x-1)}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{3-x-2}{x-1}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{-(x-1)}{x-1}=-1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Left hand derivative {tex} \neq {/tex} Right hand derivative
So, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 1.
Hence, Rolle’s theorem is not applicable on the interval [0, 2].

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the points on the curve {tex}y = \left( {\cos x - 1} \right){/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,2\pi } \right]{/tex} where the tangent is parallel to x-axis.
question_html: Find the points on the curve {tex}y = \left( {\cos x - 1} \right){/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,2\pi } \right]{/tex} where the tangent is parallel to x-axis.
solution_text: The equation of the curve is {tex}y = \cos x - 1{/tex} . Now, we have to find a point on the curve in {tex}\left[ {0,2\pi } \right]{/tex} where the tangent is parallel to X-axis i.e., the tangent to the curve at x = c has a slope 0, where {tex}c \in (0,2\pi ){/tex} Let us apply Rolle’s theorem to get the point. y = cos x – 1 is a continuous function in {tex}\left[ {0,2\pi } \right]{/tex} . [since it is a combination of cosine function and a constant function] {tex}y' = - \sin x{/tex} which exists in {tex}(0,2\pi ){/tex} Hence, y is differentiable in {tex}(0,2\pi ){/tex} {tex}y\left( 0 \right) = \cos 0 - 1 = 0{/tex} and {tex}y\left( {2\pi } \right) = \cos 2\pi - 1 = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore y\left( 0 \right) = y\left( {2\pi } \right){/tex} Since, conditions of Rolle’s theorem are satisfied. Hence, there exists a real number c {tex}\in{/tex} (0,2 {tex}\pi{/tex} ) such that {tex}f'\left( c \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - \sin c = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow c = \pi \,\,or\,\,0{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow c= {tex}\pi{/tex} \ {/tex} [ c=0 is rejected as c {tex}\in{/tex} (0,2 {tex}\pi{/tex} )] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x= {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y={/tex} cos {tex}\pi{/tex} -1=-1-1=-2 Hence, the required point on the curve, where the tangent drawn is parallel to the X-axis is {tex}\left( {\pi , - 2} \right){/tex} .
solution_html:
The equation of the curve is {tex}y = \cos x - 1{/tex}.

Now, we have to find a point on the curve in {tex}\left[ {0,2\pi } \right]{/tex}

where the tangent is parallel to X-axis i.e., the tangent to the curve at x = c has a slope 0,

where {tex}c \in (0,2\pi ){/tex}

Let us apply Rolle’s theorem to get the point.

  1. y = cos x – 1 is a continuous function in {tex}\left[ {0,2\pi } \right]{/tex}.
    [since it is a combination of cosine function and a constant function]
  2. {tex}y' = - \sin x{/tex} which exists in {tex}(0,2\pi ){/tex}
    Hence, y is differentiable in {tex}(0,2\pi ){/tex}
  3. {tex}y\left( 0 \right) = \cos 0 - 1 = 0{/tex} and {tex}y\left( {2\pi } \right) = \cos 2\pi - 1 = 0{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore y\left( 0 \right) = y\left( {2\pi } \right){/tex}
    Since, conditions of Rolle’s theorem are satisfied.
    Hence, there exists a real number c{tex}\in{/tex}(0,2{tex}\pi{/tex}) such that
    {tex}f'\left( c \right) = 0{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow - \sin c = 0{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow c = \pi \,\,or\,\,0{/tex}{tex}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow c={tex}\pi{/tex}\ {/tex}[ c=0 is rejected as c{tex}\in{/tex} (0,2{tex}\pi{/tex})]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x={tex}\pi{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow y={/tex}  cos{tex}\pi{/tex}-1=-1-1=-2

Hence, the required point on the curve, where the tangent drawn is parallel to the X-axis is {tex}\left( {\pi , - 2} \right){/tex}.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Using Rolle’s theorem, find the point on the curve {tex}y = x\left( {x - 4} \right),x \in \left[ {0,4} \right]{/tex} where the tangent is parallel to x-axis.
question_html: Using Rolle’s theorem, find the point on the curve {tex}y = x\left( {x - 4} \right),x \in \left[ {0,4} \right]{/tex}where the tangent is parallel to x-axis.
solution_text: We have, {tex}y = x\left( {x - 4} \right),x \in \left[ {0,4} \right]{/tex} y is a continuous function since {tex}x\left( {x - 4} \right){/tex} is a polynomial function. Hence, {tex}y = x\left( {x - 4} \right){/tex} is continuous in {tex}\left[ {0,4} \right]{/tex} {tex}y' = \left( {x - 4} \right).1 + x.1 = 2x - 4{/tex} , {tex}{/tex} which exists in (0, 4). Hence, y is differentiable in (0, 4). {tex}y\left( 0 \right) = 0\left( {0 - 4} \right) = 0{/tex} and {tex}y\left( 4 \right) = 4\left( {4 - 4} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y\left( 0 \right) = y\left( 4 \right){/tex} Since, conditions of Rolle’s theorem are satisfied. Hence, there exists a point c such that {tex}f'\left( c \right) = 0{/tex} in {tex}\left( {0,4} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because f'\left( x \right) = y'} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2c - 4 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow c = 2{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = 2;y = 2\left( {2 - 4} \right) = - 4{/tex} Thus, (2, -4) is the point on the curve at which the tangent drawn is parallel to X-axis.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}y = x\left( {x - 4} \right),x \in \left[ {0,4} \right]{/tex}

  1. y is a continuous function since {tex}x\left( {x - 4} \right){/tex} is a polynomial function.
    Hence, {tex}y = x\left( {x - 4} \right){/tex} is continuous in {tex}\left[ {0,4} \right]{/tex}
  2. {tex}y' = \left( {x - 4} \right).1 + x.1 = 2x - 4{/tex}, {tex}{/tex}which exists in (0, 4).
    Hence, y is differentiable in (0, 4).
  3. {tex}y\left( 0 \right) = 0\left( {0 - 4} \right) = 0{/tex}
    and {tex}y\left( 4 \right) = 4\left( {4 - 4} \right) = 0{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow y\left( 0 \right) = y\left( 4 \right){/tex}

Since, conditions of Rolle’s theorem are satisfied.

Hence, there exists a point c such that

{tex}f'\left( c \right) = 0{/tex} in {tex}\left( {0,4} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because f'\left( x \right) = y'} \right]{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 2c - 4 = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow c = 2{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow x = 2;y = 2\left( {2 - 4} \right) = - 4{/tex}

Thus, (2, -4) is the point on the curve at which the tangent drawn is parallel to X-axis.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Verify mean value theorem for the function: {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{4 x-1}{/tex} in [1, 4].
question_html: Verify mean value theorem for the function: {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{4 x-1}{/tex} in [1, 4].
solution_text: We have {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{4 x-1}{/tex} in [1, 4] f(x) is continuous in [1, 4] Also, at {tex}x=\frac{1}{4} \quad f(x){/tex} is discontinuous. Hence, f(x) is continuous in [1, 4] {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-\frac{4}{(4 x-1)^{2}},{/tex} which exists in (1, 4) Since, conditions of mean value theorem are satisfied. Hence, there exists a real number {tex}c \in\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {, 4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} such that {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(4)-f(1)}{4-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-4}{(4 c-1)^{2}}=\frac{\frac{1}{16-1}-\frac{1}{4-1}}{4-1}=\frac{\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{3}}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-4}{(4 c-1)^{2}}=\frac{1-5}{45}=\frac{-4}{45}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(4 c-1)^{2}=45{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 c-1=\pm 3 \sqrt{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow c=\frac{3 \sqrt{5}+1}{4} \in(1,4){/tex} [neglecting (-ve), value] Hence, mean value theorem has been verified.
solution_html:
We have {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{4 x-1}{/tex} in [1, 4]

  1. f(x) is continuous in [1, 4]
    Also, at {tex}x=\frac{1}{4} \quad f(x){/tex} is discontinuous.
    Hence, f(x) is continuous in [1, 4]
  2. {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-\frac{4}{(4 x-1)^{2}},{/tex} which exists in (1, 4)
    Since, conditions of mean value theorem are satisfied.
    Hence, there exists a real number {tex}c \in\left[\begin{array}{ll} {1} & {, 4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} such that
    {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(4)-f(1)}{4-1}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-4}{(4 c-1)^{2}}=\frac{\frac{1}{16-1}-\frac{1}{4-1}}{4-1}=\frac{\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{3}}{3}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-4}{(4 c-1)^{2}}=\frac{1-5}{45}=\frac{-4}{45}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow(4 c-1)^{2}=45{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow 4 c-1=\pm 3 \sqrt{5}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow c=\frac{3 \sqrt{5}+1}{4} \in(1,4){/tex} [neglecting (-ve), value]

Hence, mean value theorem has been verified.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Verify mean value theorem for the function: f (x) = x 3 – 2x 2 – x + 3 in [0, 1]
question_html: Verify mean value theorem for the function: f (x) = x3 – 2x2 – x + 3 in [0, 1]
solution_text: We have, f (x) = x 3 – 2x 2 – x + 3 in [0, 1]. Since, f(x) is a polynomial function. Hence, f(x) is continuous in [0, 1] f'(x) = 3x 2 - 4x - 1, which exists in (0, 1) Hence, f(x) is differentiable in (0, 1). Since, continuous of mean value theorem are satisfied. Therefore, by mean value theorem {tex}\exists c \in (0,1){/tex} , such that {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(1)-f(0)}{1-0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 c^{2}-4 c-1=\frac{[1-2-1+3]-[0+3]}{1-0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 c^{2}-4 c-1=\frac{-2}{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3c 2 - 4c + 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3c 2 - 3c - c + 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3c(c - 1) -1(c - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3c - 1) (c - 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow c=\frac1 3,1{/tex} , where {tex}\frac{1}{3} \in(0,1){/tex} Hence, the mean value theorem has been verified.
solution_html:
We have, f (x) = x3 – 2x2 – x + 3 in [0, 1].

  1. Since, f(x) is a polynomial function.
    Hence, f(x) is continuous in [0, 1]
  2. f'(x) = 3x2 - 4x - 1, which exists in (0, 1)
    Hence, f(x) is differentiable in (0, 1).
    Since, continuous of mean value theorem are satisfied.
    Therefore, by mean value theorem {tex}\exists c \in (0,1){/tex}, such that
    {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(1)-f(0)}{1-0}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow 3 c^{2}-4 c-1=\frac{[1-2-1+3]-[0+3]}{1-0}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow 3 c^{2}-4 c-1=\frac{-2}{1}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3c2 - 4c + 1 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3c2 - 3c - c + 1 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3c(c - 1) -1(c - 1) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3c - 1) (c - 1) = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow c=\frac1 3,1{/tex}, where {tex}\frac{1}{3} \in(0,1){/tex}

Hence, the mean value theorem has been verified.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Verify mean value theorem for the function: f(x) = sin x - sin 2x in [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ]
question_html: Verify mean value theorem for the function: f(x) = sin x - sin 2x in [0, {tex}\pi{/tex}]
solution_text: We have, f(x) = sin x - sin 2x in [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ] Since, we know that sine functions are continuous functions hence f(x) = sin x - sin 2x is a continuous function in {tex}[0, \pi]{/tex} . {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\cos x-\cos 2 x \cdot 2=\cos x-2 \cos 2 x{/tex} , which exists in {tex}(0, \pi){/tex} So, f(x) is differentiable in {tex}(0, \pi){/tex} . Continuous of mean value theorem are satisfied. Hence, {tex}\exists c \in (0,1){/tex} such that, {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(\pi)-f(0)}{\pi-0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos c-2 \cos 2 c=\frac{\sin \pi-\sin 2 \pi-\sin 0+\sin 2.0}{\pi-0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \cos 2 c-\cos c=\frac{0}{\pi}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \cdot\left(2 \cos ^{2} c-1\right)-\cos c=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \cos ^{2} c-2-\cos c=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \cos ^{2} c-\cos c-2=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos c=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1+32}}{8}=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{33}}{8}{/tex} {tex}\therefore c=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{33}}{8}\right){/tex} Also, {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{33}}{8}\right) \in(0, \pi){/tex} Hence, mean value theorem has been verified.
solution_html:
We have, f(x) = sin x - sin 2x in [0, {tex}\pi{/tex}]

  1. Since, we know that sine functions are continuous functions hence f(x) = sin x - sin 2x is a continuous function in {tex}[0, \pi]{/tex}.
  2. {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\cos x-\cos 2 x \cdot 2=\cos x-2 \cos 2 x{/tex}, which exists in {tex}(0, \pi){/tex}
    So, f(x) is differentiable in {tex}(0, \pi){/tex}. Continuous of mean value theorem are satisfied.
    Hence, {tex}\exists c \in (0,1){/tex} such that, {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(\pi)-f(0)}{\pi-0}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \cos c-2 \cos 2 c=\frac{\sin \pi-\sin 2 \pi-\sin 0+\sin 2.0}{\pi-0}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \cos 2 c-\cos c=\frac{0}{\pi}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \cdot\left(2 \cos ^{2} c-1\right)-\cos c=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \cos ^{2} c-2-\cos c=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow 4 \cos ^{2} c-\cos c-2=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \cos c=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1+32}}{8}=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{33}}{8}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore c=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{33}}{8}\right){/tex}
    Also, {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{33}}{8}\right) \in(0, \pi){/tex}

Hence, mean value theorem has been verified.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Verify mean value theorem for the function: {tex}f(x)=\sqrt{25-x^{2}} \text { in }[1,5]{/tex}
question_html: Verify mean value theorem for the function: {tex}f(x)=\sqrt{25-x^{2}} \text { in }[1,5]{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x)=\sqrt{25-x^{2}}{/tex} in [1, 5] Since, f(x) = (25 - x 2 ) 1/2 , where {tex}25-x^{2} \geq 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \leq \pm 5 \Rightarrow-5 \leq x \leq 5{/tex} Hence, f(x) is continuous in [1, 5] {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(25-x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}-2 x=-\frac{-x}{\sqrt{25-x^{2}}}{/tex} , which exists in (1, 5) Hence, f'(x) is differentiable in (1, 5). Since, conditions of mean value theorem are satisfied. By mean value theorem {tex}\exists c \in (0,1){/tex} such that {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(5)-f(1)}{5-1} \Rightarrow \frac{-c}{\sqrt{25-c^{2}}}=\frac{0-\sqrt{24}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{c^{2}}{25-c^{2}}=\frac{24}{16}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 16 c^{2}=600-24 c^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow c^{2}=\frac{600}{40}=15{/tex} {tex}\therefore c=\pm \sqrt{15}{/tex} Also, {tex}c=\sqrt{15} \in(1,5){/tex} Hence, the mean value theorem has been verified.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x)=\sqrt{25-x^{2}}{/tex} in [1, 5]

  1. Since, f(x) = (25 - x2)1/2, where {tex}25-x^{2} \geq 0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \leq \pm 5 \Rightarrow-5 \leq x \leq 5{/tex}
    Hence, f(x) is continuous in [1, 5]
  2. {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(25-x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}-2 x=-\frac{-x}{\sqrt{25-x^{2}}}{/tex}, which exists in (1, 5)
    Hence, f'(x) is differentiable in (1, 5).
    Since, conditions of mean value theorem are satisfied.
    By mean value theorem {tex}\exists c \in (0,1){/tex} such that
    {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(5)-f(1)}{5-1} \Rightarrow \frac{-c}{\sqrt{25-c^{2}}}=\frac{0-\sqrt{24}}{4}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{c^{2}}{25-c^{2}}=\frac{24}{16}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow 16 c^{2}=600-24 c^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow c^{2}=\frac{600}{40}=15{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore c=\pm \sqrt{15}{/tex}
    Also, {tex}c=\sqrt{15} \in(1,5){/tex}

Hence, the mean value theorem has been verified.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find a point on the curve y = (x - 3) 2 , where the tangent is parallel to the chord joining the points (3, 0) and (4, 1).
question_html: Find a point on the curve y = (x - 3)2, where the tangent is parallel to the chord joining the points (3, 0) and (4, 1).
solution_text: We have, y = (x - 3) 2 , which is continuous in x 1 = 3 and x 2 = 4 i.e., [3, 4] Also, {tex}y^{\prime}=2(x-3) \cdot 1=2(x-3){/tex} which exists in (3, 4) Hence, by mean value theorem, there exists a point on the curve at which tangent drawn is parallel to the chord joining the points (3, 0) and (4, 1). Thus, {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(4)-f(3)}{4-3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2(c-3)=\frac{(4-3)^{2}-(3-3)^{2}}{4-3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 c-6=\frac{1-0}{1} \Rightarrow c=\frac{7}{2}{/tex} For {tex}x=\frac{7}{2}, y=\left(\frac{7}{2}-3\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} So, {tex}\left(\frac{7}{2}, \frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} is the point on the curve at which tangent drawn is parallel to the chord joining the points (3, 0) and (4, 1).
solution_html:
We have, y = (x - 3)2, which is continuous in x1 = 3 and x2 = 4 i.e., [3, 4]
Also, {tex}y^{\prime}=2(x-3) \cdot 1=2(x-3){/tex} which exists in (3, 4)
Hence, by mean value theorem, there exists a point on the curve at which tangent drawn is parallel to the chord joining the points (3, 0) and (4, 1).
Thus, {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(4)-f(3)}{4-3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2(c-3)=\frac{(4-3)^{2}-(3-3)^{2}}{4-3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 c-6=\frac{1-0}{1} \Rightarrow c=\frac{7}{2}{/tex}
For {tex}x=\frac{7}{2}, y=\left(\frac{7}{2}-3\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
So, {tex}\left(\frac{7}{2}, \frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} is the point on the curve at which tangent drawn is parallel to the chord joining the points (3, 0) and (4, 1).

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Using mean value theorem, prove that there is a point on the curve y = 2x 2 - 5x + 3 between the points A(1, 0) and B (2, 1), where tangent is parallel to the chord AB. Also, find that point.
question_html: Using mean value theorem, prove that there is a point on the curve y = 2x2 - 5x + 3 between the points A(1, 0) and B (2, 1), where tangent is parallel to the chord AB. Also, find that point.
solution_text: We have, y = 2x 2 - 5x + 3 Which is continuous in [1, 2] as it is a polynomial function. Also, y’ = 4x – 5, which exists in (1, 2). By mean value theorem, {tex}\exists c \in (0,1){/tex} at which drawn tangent is parallel to the chord AB, where A and B are (1, 0) and (2, 1), respectively. {tex}\therefore f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(2)-f(1)}{2-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 c-5=\frac{(8-10+3)-(2-5+3)}{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4 c-5=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow c=\frac{6}{4}=\frac{3}{2} \in(1,2){/tex} For {tex}x=\frac{3}{2}, y=2\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}-5\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)+3{/tex} {tex}=2 \times \frac{9}{4}-\frac{15}{2}+3=\frac{9-15+6}{2}=0{/tex} Hence, {tex}\left(\frac{3}{2}, 0\right) {i s}{/tex} is the point on the curve y = 2x 2 - 5x + 3 be tween the points A(1, 0) and B(2, 1) where tangent is parallel to the chord AB.
solution_html:
We have, y = 2x2 - 5x + 3 Which is continuous in [1, 2] as it is a polynomial function.
Also, y’ = 4x – 5, which exists in (1, 2).
By mean value theorem, {tex}\exists c \in (0,1){/tex} at which drawn tangent is parallel to the chord AB, where A and B are (1, 0) and (2, 1), respectively.
{tex}\therefore f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(2)-f(1)}{2-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 c-5=\frac{(8-10+3)-(2-5+3)}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 c-5=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow c=\frac{6}{4}=\frac{3}{2} \in(1,2){/tex}
For {tex}x=\frac{3}{2}, y=2\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}-5\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)+3{/tex}
{tex}=2 \times \frac{9}{4}-\frac{15}{2}+3=\frac{9-15+6}{2}=0{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\left(\frac{3}{2}, 0\right) {i s}{/tex} is the point on the curve y = 2x2 - 5x + 3 be tween the points A(1, 0) and B(2, 1) where tangent is parallel to the chord AB.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the values of p and q so that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x^{2}+3 x+p,} & {\text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {q x+2} & {, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is differentiable at x = 1
question_html: Find the values of p and q so that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x^{2}+3 x+p,} & {\text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {q x+2} & {, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is differentiable at x = 1
solution_text: We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {x^{2}+3 x+p, \text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {q x+2, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is differentiable at x = 1. {tex}\therefore L f^{\prime}(1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{\left(x^{2}+3 x+p\right)-(1+3+p)}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[(1-h)^{2}+3(1-h)+p\right]-[1+3+p]}{(1-h)-1}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[1+h^{2}-2 h+3-3 h+p\right]-[4+p]}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[h^{2}-5 h+p+4-4-p\right]}{-h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h[h-5]}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow 0}-[h-5]=5{/tex} And {tex}R f^{\prime}(1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{(y x+2)-(1+3+p)}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[q(1+h)+2]-(4+p)}{1+h-1}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[q+q h+2-4-p]}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{q h+(q-2-p)}{h}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow q-2-p=0 \Rightarrow p-q=-2{/tex} .... (i) {tex}\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{q h+0}{h}=q{/tex} [for existing the limit] if Lf'(1) = Rf'(1), then 5 = q {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p - 5 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} p = 3 and q = 5
solution_html:
We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {x^{2}+3 x+p, \text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {q x+2, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is differentiable at x = 1.
{tex}\therefore L f^{\prime}(1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{\left(x^{2}+3 x+p\right)-(1+3+p)}{x-1}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[(1-h)^{2}+3(1-h)+p\right]-[1+3+p]}{(1-h)-1}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[1+h^{2}-2 h+3-3 h+p\right]-[4+p]}{-h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[h^{2}-5 h+p+4-4-p\right]}{-h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h[h-5]}{-h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow 0}-[h-5]=5{/tex}
And {tex}R f^{\prime}(1)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex}{tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{(y x+2)-(1+3+p)}{x-1}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[q(1+h)+2]-(4+p)}{1+h-1}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[q+q h+2-4-p]}{h}{/tex}{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{q h+(q-2-p)}{h}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow q-2-p=0 \Rightarrow p-q=-2{/tex} .... (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{q h+0}{h}=q{/tex} [for existing the limit]
if Lf'(1) = Rf'(1), then 5 = q
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p - 5 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} p = 3 and q = 5

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If x m y n = (x + y) m + n , prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x }{/tex} .
question_html: If xm yn = (x + y)m + n, prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x }{/tex}.
solution_text: According to the question, x m y n = (x + y) m + n On taking log both sides, we get log(x m y n )= log (x + y) m + n {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log(x m ) + log(y n )= (m + n)log (x + y) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} m log x + n log y = (m + n) log(x + y) On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex} \frac { m } { x } + \frac { n } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { m + n } { x + y } \left( 1 + \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { m } { x } + \frac { n } { y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { m + n } { x + y } + \frac { m + n } { x + y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { m } { x } - \frac { ( m + n ) } { x + y }{/tex} {tex} = \left( \frac { m + n } { x + y } - \frac { n } { y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \frac { m y + n y - n x - n y } { y ( x + y ) } \right] \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { m x + m y - m x - n x } { x ( x + y ) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } \left[ \frac { m y - n x } { y } \right] = \frac { m y - n x } { x }{/tex} Hence, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, xm yn = (x + y)m + n
On taking log both sides, we get
log(xm yn)= log (x + y)m + n
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}log(xm) + log(yn)= (m + n)log (x + y)
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} m log x + n log y = (m + n) log(x + y)
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex} \frac { m } { x } + \frac { n } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { m + n } { x + y } \left( 1 + \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { m } { x } + \frac { n } { y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}{tex} = \frac { m + n } { x + y } + \frac { m + n } { x + y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { m } { x } - \frac { ( m + n ) } { x + y }{/tex}{tex} = \left( \frac { m + n } { x + y } - \frac { n } { y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \frac { m y + n y - n x - n y } { y ( x + y ) } \right] \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}= {tex}\frac { m x + m y - m x - n x } { x ( x + y ) }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } \left[ \frac { m y - n x } { y } \right] = \frac { m y - n x } { x }{/tex}
Hence, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If x m y n = (x + y) m+n , prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 0
question_html: If xmyn = (x + y)m+n, prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 0
solution_text: We have, x m {tex}\times{/tex} y n = (x + y) m+n {tex}\therefore \quad \log \left(x^{m} \times y^{n}\right)=\log \left[(x+y)^{m+n}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m logx + n log y = (m + n)log (x + y) Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}m \frac{1}{x}+n \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=(m+n) \frac{1}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{m}{x}-\frac{m+n}{x+y}=\left(\frac{m+n}{x+y}-\frac{n}{y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{m y-n x}{x(x+y)}=\left(\frac{m y-n x}{y(x+y)}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} Differentiating above w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y \cdot 1}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{x \cdot \frac{y}{x}-y}{x^{2}}=0{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
xm{tex}\times{/tex}yn = (x + y)m+n
{tex}\therefore \quad \log \left(x^{m} \times y^{n}\right)=\log \left[(x+y)^{m+n}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m logx + n log y = (m + n)log (x + y)
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x,
{tex}m \frac{1}{x}+n \frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=(m+n) \frac{1}{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{m}{x}-\frac{m+n}{x+y}=\left(\frac{m+n}{x+y}-\frac{n}{y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{m y-n x}{x(x+y)}=\left(\frac{m y-n x}{y(x+y)}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Differentiating above w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y \cdot 1}{x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{x \cdot \frac{y}{x}-y}{x^{2}}=0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If x = sin t, y = sin pt, prove that {tex}\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}+p^2 y=0{/tex} .
question_html: If x = sin t, y = sin pt, prove that {tex}\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}+p^2 y=0{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, x = sint and y = sin pt, {tex}\therefore \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \cos pt.p{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{dy/dt}}{{dx/dt}} = \frac{{p.\cos pt}}{{\cos t}}{/tex} ...(i) Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{\cos t.\frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {p.\cos pt} \right)\frac{{dt}}{{dx}} - p\cos pt.\frac{d}{{dt}}\cos t.\frac{{dt}}{{dx}}}}{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left[ {\cos t.p.\left( { - \sin pt} \right).p - p\cos pt.\left( { - \sin t} \right)} \right]\frac{{dt}}{{dx}}}}{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left[ { - {p^2}\sin pt.\cos t + p\ sint.cospt} \right].\frac{1}{{\cos t}}}}{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{ - {p^2}\sin pt.\cos t + p\cos pt.\sin t}}{{{{\cos }^3}t}}{/tex} ...(ii) Since, we have to prove {tex}\left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + {p^2}y = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore LHS = \left( {1 - {{\sin }^2}t} \right)\frac{{\left[- {{p^2}\ sinpt.\ cost + p\ cospt .\sin t} \right]}}{{{{\cos }^3}t}}{/tex} {tex}- \ sint .\frac{{p\cos pt}}{{\cos t}} + {p^2}\sin pt{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{{{\cos }^3}t}}\left[ \begin{gathered} \left( {1 - {{\sin }^2}t} \right)\left( { - {p^2}\sin pt.\cos t + p\cos pt.\sin t} \right) \hfill \\ - p\cos pt.\sin t.{\cos ^2}t + {p^2}\sin pt.{\cos ^3}t \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{{{\cos }^3}t}}\left[ \begin{gathered} - {p^2}\sin pt.{\cos ^3}t + p\cos pt.\sin t.{\cos ^2}t \hfill \\ - p\cos pt.\sin t.{\cos ^2}t + {p^2}\sin pt.{\cos ^3}t \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because 1 - {{\sin }^2}t = {{\cos }^2}t} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{{{\cos }^3}t}}.0{/tex} = 0 Hence proved.
solution_html:
We have, x = sint and y = sin pt,
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \cos pt.p{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{dy/dt}}{{dx/dt}} = \frac{{p.\cos pt}}{{\cos t}}{/tex} ...(i)
Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{\cos t.\frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {p.\cos pt} \right)\frac{{dt}}{{dx}} - p\cos pt.\frac{d}{{dt}}\cos t.\frac{{dt}}{{dx}}}}{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\left[ {\cos t.p.\left( { - \sin pt} \right).p - p\cos pt.\left( { - \sin t} \right)} \right]\frac{{dt}}{{dx}}}}{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\left[ { - {p^2}\sin pt.\cos t + p\ sint.cospt} \right].\frac{1}{{\cos t}}}}{{{{\cos }^2}t}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{ - {p^2}\sin pt.\cos t + p\cos pt.\sin t}}{{{{\cos }^3}t}}{/tex} ...(ii)
Since, we have to prove
{tex}\left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + {p^2}y = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore LHS = \left( {1 - {{\sin }^2}t} \right)\frac{{\left[- {{p^2}\ sinpt.\ cost + p\ cospt .\sin t} \right]}}{{{{\cos }^3}t}}{/tex}
{tex}- \ sint .\frac{{p\cos pt}}{{\cos t}} + {p^2}\sin pt{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{{{{\cos }^3}t}}\left[ \begin{gathered} \left( {1 - {{\sin }^2}t} \right)\left( { - {p^2}\sin pt.\cos t + p\cos pt.\sin t} \right) \hfill \\ - p\cos pt.\sin t.{\cos ^2}t + {p^2}\sin pt.{\cos ^3}t \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{1}{{{{\cos }^3}t}}\left[ \begin{gathered} - {p^2}\sin pt.{\cos ^3}t + p\cos pt.\sin t.{\cos ^2}t \hfill \\ - p\cos pt.\sin t.{\cos ^2}t + {p^2}\sin pt.{\cos ^3}t \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}{tex}\left[ {\because 1 - {{\sin }^2}t = {{\cos }^2}t} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{{{{\cos }^3}t}}.0{/tex}
= 0 Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x},{/tex} if y {tex}=x^{\tan x}+\sqrt{\frac{x^{2}+1}{2}}{/tex}
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x},{/tex} if y {tex}=x^{\tan x}+\sqrt{\frac{x^{2}+1}{2}}{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , if {tex}y=x^{\operatorname{sin} x}+\sqrt{\frac{x^{2}+1}{2}}{/tex} Taking, u = xtan x and {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{x^{2}+1}{2}}{/tex} log u = tan x log x .... (i) and {tex}v^{2}=\frac{x^{2}+1}{2}{/tex} .... (ii) On, differentiating eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}=\tan x \cdot \frac{1}{x}+\log x \sec ^{2} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\frac{\tan x}{x}+\log x \cdot \sec ^{2} x\right]{/tex} {tex}=x^{\operatorname{sin} x}\left[\frac{\tan x}{x}+\log x \cdot \sec ^{2} x\right]{/tex} .... (iii) also, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}2 v \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}(2 x) \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{4 v} \cdot(2 x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{4 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{x^{2}+1}{2}}} \cdot 2 x=\frac{x \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2 \sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}}{/tex} .... (iv) Now, y = u + v {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=x^{\operatorname{tan} x}\left[\frac{\tan x}{x}+\log x \cdot \sec ^{2} x\right]+\frac{x}{\sqrt{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}, if {tex}y=x^{\operatorname{sin} x}+\sqrt{\frac{x^{2}+1}{2}}{/tex}
Taking, u = xtan x and {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{x^{2}+1}{2}}{/tex}
log u = tan x log x .... (i)
and {tex}v^{2}=\frac{x^{2}+1}{2}{/tex} .... (ii)
On, differentiating eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}=\tan x \cdot \frac{1}{x}+\log x \sec ^{2} x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\frac{\tan x}{x}+\log x \cdot \sec ^{2} x\right]{/tex}
{tex}=x^{\operatorname{sin} x}\left[\frac{\tan x}{x}+\log x \cdot \sec ^{2} x\right]{/tex} .... (iii)
also, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}2 v \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}(2 x) \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{4 v} \cdot(2 x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{4 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{x^{2}+1}{2}}} \cdot 2 x=\frac{x \cdot \sqrt{2}}{2 \sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}}{/tex} .... (iv)
Now, y = u + v
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}=x^{\operatorname{tan} x}\left[\frac{\tan x}{x}+\log x \cdot \sec ^{2} x\right]+\frac{x}{\sqrt{2\left(x^{2}+1\right)}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If f (x) = 2x and {tex}g(x)=\frac{x^{2}}{2}+1{/tex} , then which of the following can be a discontinuous function
question_html: If f (x) = 2x and {tex}g(x)=\frac{x^{2}}{2}+1{/tex}, then which of the following can be a discontinuous function

options.explanation: We know that, if f and g be continuous functions, then g is continuous at these points, where {tex}g(x) \ne 0{/tex} Here, {tex}\frac{{g(x)}}{{f(x)}} = \frac{{\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} + 1}}{{2x}} = \frac{{{x^2} + 2}}{{4x}}{/tex} Which is discontinuous at x = 0.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: The function {tex}f(x)=\frac{4-x^{2}}{4 x-x^{3}}{/tex} is
question_html: The function {tex}f(x)=\frac{4-x^{2}}{4 x-x^{3}}{/tex} is

options.explanation: We have, {tex}f(x)=\frac{4-x^{2}}{4 x-x^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(4-x^{2}\right)}{x\left(4-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(4-x^{2}\right)}{x\left(2^{2}-x^{2}\right)}=\frac{4-x^{2}}{x(2+x)(2-x)}{/tex} Clearly, f(x) is discontinuous at exactly three points x = 0, x = -2 and x = 2.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: The set of points where the function f given by f (x) = {tex}|2 x-1|{/tex} sin x is differentiable is
question_html: The set of points where the function f given by f (x) = {tex}|2 x-1|{/tex} sin x is differentiable is

options.explanation: We have, {tex}f(x)=|2 x-1| \sin x{/tex} At {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , f(x) is not differentiable Hence, f(x), is differentiable in {tex}\mathbf{R}-\left\{\frac{1}{2}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\because R f^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\underset{h\rightarrow0}{lim}~~ \frac{f\left(\frac{1}{2}+h\right)-f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left|2\left(\frac{1}{2}+h\right)-1\right| \sin \left(\frac{1}{2}+h\right)-0}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{|2 h| \cdot \sin \left(\frac{1+2 h}{2}\right)}{h}=2 \sin \frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}L f^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\underset{h\rightarrow0}{lim} \frac{f\left(\frac{1}{2}-h\right)-f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left|2\left(\frac{1}{2}-h\right){-1}\right| \sin \left(\frac{1}{2}-h\right)-0}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{|0-2 h|.\sin \left(\frac{1}{2}-h\right)}{-h}=-2 \sin \left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} Clearly, {tex} R f^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \neq L f^{\prime}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} So, f(x) is not differentiable at {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

question_text: The function f(x) = cot x is discontinuous on the set
question_html: The function f(x) = cot x is discontinuous on the set

options.explanation: We have f(x) = cot x is continuous in {tex}R-\{n \pi: n \in Z\}{/tex} Since, f(x) {tex}=\cot x=\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}{/tex} [since, sin x = 0 at {tex}n \pi, n \in Z{/tex} ] Hence, f(x) = cot x is discontinuous on the set {tex}\{x=n \pi: n \in Z\}{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: The function {tex}f(x) = {e^{|x|}}{/tex}
question_html: The function {tex}f(x) = {e^{|x|}}{/tex}

options.explanation: We have, f(x) = e |x| = {tex}\begin{cases}e^x, & x \geq 0 \\ e^{-x}, & x<0\end{cases} {/tex} LHD = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{-(0-h)}-e^0}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^h-1}{-h} {/tex} = -1 and RHD = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{0+h}-f(0)}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^h-1}{h}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHD {tex}\neq{/tex} RHD So, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 0. Hence, f(x) is differentiable everywhere except at x = 0.

question_text: If {tex}f(x)=x^{2} \sin \frac{1}{x}{/tex} where {tex}x \neq 0{/tex} then the value of the function f at x = 0, so that the function is continuous at x = 0, is
question_html: If {tex}f(x)=x^{2} \sin \frac{1}{x}{/tex}where {tex}x \neq 0{/tex} then the value of the function f at x = 0, so that the function is continuous at x = 0, is

options.explanation: We have, {tex} f(x)=x^{2} \sin \left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} , where x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 Since the function is continuous at x = 0, we have f(0) = {tex}\underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}{/tex} {tex}x^{2} \sin \left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex}-1 \le sin \frac 1x \le 1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-x^2 \le x^2sin \frac 1x \le x^2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}(-x^2) \le \underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}(x^2sin \frac 1x) \le \underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}(x^2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow0 \le \underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}(x^2sin \frac 1x) \le 0{/tex} Therefore by squeeze principle, we have f(0) = {tex} \underset{x\rightarrow0}{lim}~(x^2sin \frac 1x) = 0{/tex} Hence, value of the function f at x=0 so that it is continuous at x=0 is 0.

question_text: If {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {m x+1,} & {\text { if } x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}} \\ {\sin x+n,} & {\text { if } x>\frac{\pi}{2}} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} then
question_html: If {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {m x+1,} & {\text { if } x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}} \\ {\sin x+n,} & {\text { if } x>\frac{\pi}{2}} \end{array}\right.{/tex}is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}then

options.explanation: We have, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {m x+1,} & {\text { if } x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}} \\ {\sin x+n,} & {\text { if } x>\frac{\pi}{2}} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore LHL=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2}}(m x+1)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[m\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)+1\right]=\frac{m \pi}{2}+1{/tex} and {tex}R H L=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{\prime}}{2}}(\sin x+n)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow \infty}\left[\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+h\right)+n\right]{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{n \rightarrow 0} \cos h+n=1+n{/tex} Since the function is continuous, we have {tex}L H L=R H L{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow m \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}+1=n+1{/tex} {tex}\therefore~ n=m \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

Hence, f(x) = vou(x) is also a continuous function but v(x) is not differentiable at x = 0 So, f(x) is not differentiable where sin x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = n {tex}\pi{/tex} , n {tex}\in{/tex} Z Hence, f(x) is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at x = n {tex}\pi{/tex} , n {tex}\in{/tex} Z

question_text: If {tex}y=\log \left(\frac{1-x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is equal to
question_html: If {tex}y=\log \left(\frac{1-x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex}then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}is equal to

options.explanation: We have, {tex}y=\log \left(\frac{1-x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\frac{1-x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}} \times \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{1-x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1+x^{2}}{1-x^2} \times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\left[\left(1+x^{2}\right)(-2 x)-\left(1-x^{2}\right)(+2 x)\right]}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left(1+x^{2}\right) }{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\left[-2 x-2 x^{3}-2 x+2 x^{3}\right]}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\frac{1-x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{1-x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{-2 x\left[1+x^{2}+1-x^{2}\right]}{\left(1-x^{2}\right) \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right)}=\frac{-4 x}{1-x^{4}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1 \times -4x}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-4 x}{1-x^{4}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}y=\sqrt{\sin x+y}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is equal to
question_html: If {tex}y=\sqrt{\sin x+y}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}is equal to

options.explanation: {tex}\because y=(\sin x+y)^{1/2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}(\sin x+y)^{-1 / 2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x+y){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{(\sin x+y)^{1/2}} \cdot\left(\cos x+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 y}\left(\cos x+\frac{d y}{d x}\right)\left[\because(\sin x+y)^{1 / 2}=y\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left(1-\frac{1}{2 y}\right)=\frac{\cos x}{2 y}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\cos x}{2 y} \cdot \frac{2 y}{2 y-1}=\frac{\cos x}{2 y-1}{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: The derivative of {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(2 x^{2}-1\right){/tex} w.r.t. {tex}\cos ^{-1} x{/tex} is
question_html: The derivative of {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(2 x^{2}-1\right){/tex}w.r.t. {tex}\cos ^{-1} x{/tex} is

options.explanation: let {tex}u=\cos ^{-1}\left(2 x^{2}-1\right){/tex} and {tex}v=\cos ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-\left(2 x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}} .4 x=\frac{-4 x}{\sqrt{1-\left(4 x^{4}+1-4 x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-4 x}{\sqrt{-4 x^{4}+4 x^{2}}}=\frac{-4 x}{\sqrt{4 x^{2}\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u / d x}{d v / d x}=\frac{-2 / \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{-1 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=2{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}x=t^{2}, y=t^{3}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} is
question_html: If {tex}x=t^{2}, y=t^{3}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} is

options.explanation: We have, {tex}x=t^{2}, y=t^{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d x}{d t}=2 t \text { and } \frac{d y}{d t}=3 t^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y / d t}{d x / d t}=\frac{3 t^{2}}{2 t}=\frac{3}{2} t{/tex} On further differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{d}{d t} t \cdot \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2 t}\left[\because \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 t}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{4 t}{/tex}

question_text: The value of c in Rolle’s theorem for the function f(x) = x 3 – 3x in the interval {tex}[0, \sqrt{3}]{/tex} is
question_html: The value of c in Rolle’s theorem for the function f(x) = x3 – 3x in the interval {tex}[0, \sqrt{3}]{/tex}is

options.explanation: We have {tex} f^{\prime}(c)=0 ~~{/tex} Here, {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^{2}-3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 c^{2}-3=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow c^{2}=\frac{3}{3}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow c=\pm 1, \text { where } 1 \in(0, \sqrt{3}){/tex} {tex}\therefore c=1{/tex}

question_text: For the function {tex}f(x)=x+\frac{1}{x}, x \in[1,3],{/tex} the value of c for mean value theorem is
question_html: For the function {tex}f(x)=x+\frac{1}{x}, x \in[1,3],{/tex} the value of c for mean value theorem is

options.explanation: {tex}\because f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\frac{1}{c^{2}}=\frac{\left[3+\frac{1}{3}\right]-\left[1+\frac{1}{1}\right]}{3-1}\left[\begin{array}{c} {\because f^{\prime}(x)=1-\frac{1}{x^{2}}} \\ {\text { and } b=3, a=1} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{c^{2}-1}{c^{2}}=\frac{\frac{10}{3}-2}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{c^{2}-1}{c^{2}}=\frac{4}{3 \times 2}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3\left(c^{2}-1\right)=2 c^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 c^{2}-2 c^{2}=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow c^{2}=3 \Rightarrow c=\pm \sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\because c=\sqrt{3} \in(1,3){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the value of the constant k so that the function f defined below is continuous at {tex}x = 0,{/tex} Where {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{8{x^2}}},x \ne 0 \hfill \\ k,\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
question_html: Find the value of the constant k so that the function f defined below is continuous at {tex}x = 0,{/tex} Where {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{8{x^2}}},x \ne 0 \hfill \\ k,\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that the function f is continuous at x = 0. Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f\left( x \right) = f\left( 0 \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{8{x^2}}} = k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}2x}}{{8{x^2}}} = k\ \left(\therefore {1 - \cos 2x = 2{{\sin }^2}x} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {\left( {\frac{{\sin 2x}}{{2x}}} \right)^2}=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k = 1{/tex} {tex}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that the function f is continuous at x = 0.
Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f\left( x \right) = f\left( 0 \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{8{x^2}}} = k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}2x}}{{8{x^2}}} = k\ \left(\therefore {1 - \cos 2x = 2{{\sin }^2}x} \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {\left( {\frac{{\sin 2x}}{{2x}}} \right)^2}=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow k = 1{/tex}{tex}{/tex}

question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function: {tex}f(x) = \sin x.\cos x{/tex}
solution_text: Since sin x and cos x are continuous functions and product of two continuous function is a continuous function, therefore {tex}f(x) = \sin x.\cos x{/tex} is a continuous function.

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{x^3} + {x^2} - 16x + 20}}{{{{\left( {x - 2} \right)}^2}}},x \ne 2 \hfill \\ k,x = 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 2, find the value of k.
question_html: If {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{x^3} + {x^2} - 16x + 20}}{{{{\left( {x - 2} \right)}^2}}},x \ne 2 \hfill \\ k,x = 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 2, find the value of k.
solution_text: Given {tex}f(2) = k.{/tex} . Now, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{{x^3} + {x^2} - 16x + 20}}{{{{\left( {x - 2} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{\left( {x + 5} \right){{\left( {x - 2} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {x - 2} \right)}^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \left( {x + 5} \right) = 7{/tex} As f is continuous at x= 2, we have {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} f(x) = f(2){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow k = 7{/tex}
solution_html:
Given {tex}f(2) = k.{/tex}.

Now, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{{x^3} + {x^2} - 16x + 20}}{{{{\left( {x - 2} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{\left( {x + 5} \right){{\left( {x - 2} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {x - 2} \right)}^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \left( {x + 5} \right) = 7{/tex}

As f is continuous at x= 2, we have

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} f(x) = f(2){/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow k = 7{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Show that the function f defined by {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x\sin \frac{1}{x},x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
question_html: Show that the function f defined by {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x\sin \frac{1}{x},x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
solution_text: Left hand limit at x = 0 is given by {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} x\sin \frac{1}{x} = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{\text{since, - 1 < sin }}\frac{1}{x} < 1} \right]{/tex} Similarly, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ +}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} x\sin \frac{1}{x} = 0{/tex} . Moreover f(0) = 0. Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = f(0){/tex} Hence f is Continous at x = 0.
solution_html:
Left hand limit at x = 0 is given by

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} x\sin \frac{1}{x} = 0{/tex}{tex}\left[ {{\text{since, - 1 < sin }}\frac{1}{x} < 1} \right]{/tex}

Similarly, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ +}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} x\sin \frac{1}{x} = 0{/tex}. Moreover f(0) = 0. 

Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = f(0){/tex}

Hence f is Continous at x = 0.

question_text: Given {tex}f(x) = \frac{1}{{x - 1}}{/tex} . Find the points of discontinuity of the composite function y = f[f(x)].
question_html: Given {tex}f(x) = \frac{1}{{x - 1}}{/tex}. Find the points of discontinuity of the composite function y = f[f(x)].
solution_text: We know that {tex}f\left( x \right) = \frac{1}{{x - 1}}{/tex} is discontinuous at x = 1 Now, for {tex}x \ne 1{/tex} , {tex}f\left( {f\left( x \right)} \right) = f\left( {\frac{1}{{x - 1}}} \right) = \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{{x - 1}} - 1}} = \frac{{x - 1}}{{2 - x}}{/tex} Which is discontinuous at x = 2. Hence, the point of discontinuity are x = 1 and x = 2.
solution_html:
We know that {tex}f\left( x \right) = \frac{1}{{x - 1}}{/tex} is discontinuous at x = 1
Now, for {tex}x \ne 1{/tex},
{tex}f\left( {f\left( x \right)} \right) = f\left( {\frac{1}{{x - 1}}} \right) = \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{{x - 1}} - 1}} = \frac{{x - 1}}{{2 - x}}{/tex}
Which is discontinuous at x = 2.
Hence, the point of discontinuity are x = 1 and x = 2.

question_text: Let f(x) = x|x|, for all {tex}x \in R{/tex} check its differentiability at x = 0.
question_html: Let f(x) = x|x|, for all {tex}x \in R{/tex} check its differentiability at x = 0.
solution_text: f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{cc} -\text x^2, &\text x<0 \\ \text x^2, & \text x \geq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} , differentiable at 0.
solution_html: f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{cc} -\text x^2, &\text x<0 \\ \text x^2, & \text x \geq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}, differentiable at 0.

question_text: Differentiate {tex}\sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } {/tex} with respect to x.
question_html: Differentiate {tex}\sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } {/tex} with respect to x.
solution_text: Let {tex}y = \sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } {/tex} . Using chain rule, we get {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } }}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } }}.{\sec ^2}\sqrt x \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\sqrt x } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } }}.\left( {{{\sec }^2}\sqrt x } \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{2\sqrt x }}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {{{\sec }^2}\sqrt x } \right)}}{{4\sqrt x \sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } }}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y = \sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } {/tex} .

Using chain rule, we get

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } }}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right){/tex}

{tex} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } }}.{\sec ^2}\sqrt x \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\sqrt x } \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } }}.\left( {{{\sec }^2}\sqrt x } \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{2\sqrt x }}} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\left( {{{\sec }^2}\sqrt x } \right)}}{{4\sqrt x \sqrt {\tan \sqrt x } }}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If y = tan(x + y), find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
question_html: If y = tan(x + y), find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
solution_text: Given y = tan (x + y). differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we have {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sec ^{2}(x+y) \frac{d}{d x}(x+y){/tex} {tex}=\sec ^{2}(x+y)\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left[1-\sec ^{2}(x+y) ] \frac{d y}{d x}=\sec ^{2}(x+y)\right.{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sec ^{2}(x+y)}{1-\sec ^{2}(x+y)}=-cosec ^{2}(x+y){/tex}
solution_html:
Given y = tan (x + y). differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we have
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sec ^{2}(x+y) \frac{d}{d x}(x+y){/tex}
{tex}=\sec ^{2}(x+y)\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
or {tex}\left[1-\sec ^{2}(x+y) ] \frac{d y}{d x}=\sec ^{2}(x+y)\right.{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sec ^{2}(x+y)}{1-\sec ^{2}(x+y)}=-cosec ^{2}(x+y){/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If e x + e y = e x+y , prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-e^{y-x}{/tex} .
question_html: If ex + ey = ex+y, prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-e^{y-x}{/tex}.
solution_text: Given that e x + e y = e x+y {tex}\Rightarrow e^{x}+e^{y}=e^{x} \cdot e^{y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{-y}+e^{-x}=1{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we have {tex}(-e^{-y } )\frac{d y}{d x}-e^{-x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-e^{y} \cdot e^{-x}=-e^{y-x}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that e x + e y = e x+y 
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{x}+e^{y}=e^{x} \cdot e^{y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{-y}+e^{-x}=1{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we have
{tex}(-e^{-y } )\frac{d y}{d x}-e^{-x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-e^{y} \cdot e^{-x}=-e^{y-x}{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}},{/tex} if {tex}y = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{3x - {x^3}}}{{1 - 3{x^2}}}} \right), - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} < x < \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}},{/tex} if {tex}y = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{3x - {x^3}}}{{1 - 3{x^2}}}} \right), - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} < x < \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
solution_text: Put {tex}x = \tan \theta {/tex} , where {tex}\frac{{ - \pi }}{6} < \theta < \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}y = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{3\tan \theta - {{\tan }^3}\theta }}{{1 - 3{{\tan }^2}\theta }}} \right){/tex} {tex} = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\tan 3\theta } \right){/tex} {tex}= 3\theta \left( {\because \frac{{ - \pi }}{2} < 3\theta < \frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 3{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{3}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Put {tex}x = \tan \theta {/tex}, where {tex}\frac{{ - \pi }}{6} < \theta < \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex}

Therefore, {tex}y = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{3\tan \theta - {{\tan }^3}\theta }}{{1 - 3{{\tan }^2}\theta }}} \right){/tex}

{tex} = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\tan 3\theta } \right){/tex}

{tex}= 3\theta \left( {\because \frac{{ - \pi }}{2} < 3\theta < \frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}

{tex} = 3{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex}

Hence, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{3}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If y = {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left. {\left\{ {x\sqrt {1 - x} - \sqrt x \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right.} \right\}{/tex} and 0< x < 1, then find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} .
question_html: If y = {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left. {\left\{ {x\sqrt {1 - x} - \sqrt x \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right.} \right\}{/tex}and 0< x < 1, then find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}.
solution_text: According to the question, if, {tex}y=\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ x \sqrt { 1 - x } - \sqrt { x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} . We have to find the derivative of y w.r.t x.We shall make use of the substitution to find the derivative. Now,y can be rewritten as {tex}y=\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ x \sqrt { 1 - ( \sqrt { x } ) ^ { 2 } } - \sqrt { x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} Now, put {tex}\sqrt { x } = \sin \theta{/tex} and x = {tex}\sin \phi{/tex} , so that y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \sin \phi \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } - \sin \theta \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } [ \sin \phi \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \phi ]{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } x } = \cos x \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \sin ( \phi - \theta ){/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin \phi \cos \theta - \cos \phi \sin \theta = \sin ( \phi - \theta ) ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \phi - \theta{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sin ^ { - 1 } \sin \theta = \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } x - \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \phi = \sin ^ { - 1 } x \text { and } \theta = \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - ( \sqrt { x } ) ^ { 2 } } } \times \frac { d } { d x } ( \sqrt { x } ){/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } \theta \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - \theta ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x } } \cdot \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x } }{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, if, {tex}y=\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ x \sqrt { 1 - x } - \sqrt { x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}.

We have to find the derivative of y w.r.t x.We shall make use of the substitution to find the derivative.
Now,y  can be  rewritten as {tex}y=\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ x \sqrt { 1 - ( \sqrt { x } ) ^ { 2 } } - \sqrt { x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
Now, put {tex}\sqrt { x } = \sin \theta{/tex} and x ={tex}\sin \phi{/tex}, so that
y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \sin \phi \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } - \sin \theta \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } [ \sin \phi \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \phi ]{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } x } = \cos x \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \sin ( \phi - \theta ){/tex}{tex}[ \because \sin \phi \cos \theta - \cos \phi \sin \theta = \sin ( \phi - \theta ) ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \phi - \theta{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \sin ^ { - 1 } \sin \theta = \theta \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } x - \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \phi = \sin ^ { - 1 } x \text { and } \theta = \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } \right]{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - ( \sqrt { x } ) ^ { 2 } } } \times \frac { d } { d x } ( \sqrt { x } ){/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } \theta \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - \theta ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x } } \cdot \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x } }{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If x = a sec 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = a tan 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} .
question_html: If x = a sec3 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = a tan3 {tex}\theta{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}.
solution_text: We have x = a sec 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = a tan 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} . Differentiating w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=3 a \sec ^{2} \theta \frac{d}{d \theta}(\sec \theta)=3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \frac{d}{d \theta}(\tan \theta)=3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta}{3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta}=\frac{\tan \theta}{\sec \theta}=\sin \theta{/tex} Hence, {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{at \ \theta=\frac{\pi}{3}}=\sin \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have x = a sec3 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = a tan3 {tex}\theta{/tex}.
Differentiating w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex}, we get
{tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=3 a \sec ^{2} \theta \frac{d}{d \theta}(\sec \theta)=3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta{/tex}
and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \frac{d}{d \theta}(\tan \theta)=3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta}{3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta}=\frac{\tan \theta}{\sec \theta}=\sin \theta{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{at \ \theta=\frac{\pi}{3}}=\sin \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If x y = e x-y , then prove that {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log x } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .
question_html: If xy = ex-y, then prove that {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log x } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}.
solution_text: According to the question, {tex}x^y = e^{x-y}{/tex} Taking log both sides , {tex}\Rightarrow y log_ex = (x - y)log_ee{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y log_ex = (x - y){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y(1 + logx) = x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { x } { 1 + \log x }{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) - x \frac { d } { d x } ( 1 + \log x ) } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [ Using quotient rule of derivative] {tex}= \frac { 1 + \log x - x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 1 + \log x - 1 } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log x } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Hence Proved
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}x^y = e^{x-y}{/tex}
Taking log both sides ,
{tex}\Rightarrow y log_ex = (x - y)log_ee{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y log_ex = (x - y){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow y(1 + logx) = x{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { x } { 1 + \log x }{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) - x \frac { d } { d x } ( 1 + \log x ) } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}[ Using quotient rule of derivative]
{tex}= \frac { 1 + \log x - x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}= {tex}\frac { 1 + \log x - 1 } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log x } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Hence Proved

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If y = tan x + sec x, prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\cos x}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}{/tex}
question_html: If y = tan x + sec x, prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\cos x}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}{/tex}
solution_text: We have y = tan x + sec x. Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sec ^{2} x+\sec x \tan x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}=\frac{1+\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1+\sin x}{(1+\sin x)(1-\sin x)}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1-\sin x}{/tex} Now, differentiating again w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{-(-\cos x)}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}=\frac{\cos x}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have y = tan x + sec x. Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sec ^{2} x+\sec x \tan x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}=\frac{1+\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{1+\sin x}{(1+\sin x)(1-\sin x)}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1-\sin x}{/tex}
Now, differentiating again w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{-(-\cos x)}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}=\frac{\cos x}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}{/tex}
