question_text: If {tex}f(x) = \left| {\cos x} \right|{/tex} find {tex}f'\left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex} .
question_html: If {tex}f(x) = \left| {\cos x} \right|{/tex} find {tex}f'\left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{4}} \right){/tex}.
solution_text: When {tex}\frac{\pi }{2} < x < \pi {/tex} , {tex}\cos x < 0{/tex} so that {tex}\left| {\cos x|= - \cos x,} \right.{/tex} i.e., {tex}f(x) = - \cos x \Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = \sin x{/tex} Hence, {tex}f'\left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{4}} \right) = \sin \left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{4}} \right) = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex}
solution_html:
When {tex}\frac{\pi }{2} < x < \pi {/tex}, {tex}\cos x < 0{/tex} so that {tex}\left| {\cos x|= - \cos x,} \right.{/tex} i.e., {tex}f(x) = - \cos x \Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = \sin x{/tex}

Hence, {tex}f'\left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{4}} \right) = \sin \left( {\frac{{3\pi }}{4}} \right) = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}f(x) = \left| {\cos x - \sin x|} \right.{/tex} .find {tex}f'(\frac{\pi }{6}){/tex}
question_html: If {tex}f(x) = \left| {\cos x - \sin x|} \right.{/tex} .find {tex}f'(\frac{\pi }{6}){/tex}
solution_text: When {tex}0 < x < \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} ,cos x > sin x, so that cos x – sin x > 0, i.e., {tex}f(x) = \cos x - \sin x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = - \sin x - \cos x{/tex} Hence, {tex}f'(\frac{\pi }{6}) = - \sin \frac{\pi }{6}-cos\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{6}=-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{\sqrt3}{2} = - \frac{1}{2}\left( {1 + \sqrt 3 } \right){/tex}
solution_html:
When {tex}0 < x < \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} ,cos x > sin x, so that cos x – sin x > 0, i.e.,

{tex}f(x) = \cos x - \sin x{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) = - \sin x - \cos x{/tex}

Hence, {tex}f'(\frac{\pi }{6}) = - \sin \frac{\pi }{6}-cos\frac{{tex}\pi{/tex}}{6}=-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{\sqrt3}{2} = - \frac{1}{2}\left( {1 + \sqrt 3 } \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Verify Rolle’s theorem for the function, f (x) = sin 2x in {tex}\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}
question_html: Verify Rolle’s theorem for the function, f (x) = sin 2x in {tex}\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}
solution_text: Consider f(x) = sin 2x in {tex}\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} . Note that: The function f is continuous in {tex}\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} , as f is a sine function, which always continuous. f ′ (x) = 2cos 2x, exists in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} , hence f is derivable in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} . f(0) = sin0 = 0 and f {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\sin \pi=0 \Rightarrow f(0)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} . Conditions of Rolle’s theorem are satisfied. Hence there exists at least one {tex}c \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} such that f'(c) = 0. Thus 2 cos 2c = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow 2 c=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow c=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider f(x) = sin 2x in {tex}\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}. Note that:

  1. The function f is continuous in {tex}\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}, as f is a sine function, which always continuous.
  2. f ′ (x) = 2cos 2x, exists in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}, hence f is derivable in {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}.
  3. f(0) = sin0 = 0 and f {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\sin \pi=0 \Rightarrow f(0)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}.

Conditions of Rolle’s theorem are satisfied. Hence there exists at least one {tex}c \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} such that f'(c) = 0. Thus
2 cos 2c = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 c=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow c=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Verify mean value theorem for the function f (x) = (x – 3) (x – 6) (x – 9) in [3, 5].
question_html: Verify mean value theorem for the function f (x) = (x – 3) (x – 6) (x – 9) in [3, 5].
solution_text: Function f is continuous in [3, 5] as product of polynomial functions is a polynomial, which is continuous. f′(x) = 3x 2 - 36x + 99 exists in (3, 5) and hence derivable in (3, 5). Thus conditions of mean value theorem are satisfied. Hence, there exists at least one c {tex} \in{/tex} (3, 5) such that {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(5)-f(3)}{5-3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 c^{2}-36 c+99=\frac{8-0}{2}=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow c=6 \pm \sqrt{\frac{13}{3}}{/tex} Hence c {tex}=6-\sqrt{\frac{13}{3}}{/tex} (since other value is not permissible).
solution_html:
1. Function f is continuous in [3, 5] as product of polynomial functions is a polynomial, which is continuous.
  2. f′(x) = 3x2 - 36x + 99 exists in (3, 5) and hence derivable in (3, 5).
    Thus conditions of mean value theorem are satisfied. Hence, there exists at least one c {tex} \in{/tex} (3, 5) such that
    {tex}f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(5)-f(3)}{5-3}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow 3 c^{2}-36 c+99=\frac{8-0}{2}=4{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow c=6 \pm \sqrt{\frac{13}{3}}{/tex}

Hence c {tex}=6-\sqrt{\frac{13}{3}}{/tex} (since other value is not permissible).

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}f(x) = \frac{{\sqrt 2 \cos x - 1}}{{\cot x - 1}},x \ne \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} find the value of {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} so that f(x) becomes continuous at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex}
question_html: If {tex}f(x) = \frac{{\sqrt 2 \cos x - 1}}{{\cot x - 1}},x \ne \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} find the value of {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} so that f(x) becomes continuous at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex}
solution_text: Given, {tex}f(x) = \frac{{\sqrt 2 \cos x - 1}}{{\cot x - 1}},x \ne \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} |Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{\sqrt 2 \cos x - 1}}{{\cot x - 1}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{\left( {\sqrt 2 \cos x - 1} \right)\sin x}}{{\cos x - \sin x}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{\left( {\sqrt 2 \cos x - 1} \right)}}{{\left( {\cos x - sinx} \right)}}.\frac{{\left( {\sqrt 2 \cos x + 1} \right)}}{{\left( {\sqrt 2 \cos x + 1} \right)}}.\frac{{\left( {\cos x + \sin x} \right)}}{{\left( {\cos x + \sin x} \right)}}.\sin x{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{2{{\cos }^2}x - 1}}{{{{\cos }^2}x - {{\sin }^2}x}}.\frac{{\cos x + \sin x}}{{\sqrt 2 \cos x + 1}}.\left( {\sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{\cos 2x}}{{\cos 2x}}.\left( {\frac{{\cos x + \sin x}}{{\sqrt 2 \cos x + 1}}} \right).\left( {\sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{\left( {\cos x + \sin x} \right)}}{{\sqrt 2 \cos x + 1}}\sin x{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)}}{{\sqrt 2 .\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} + 1}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} f(x) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} If we define {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right) = \frac{1}{2},{/tex} then f(x) will become continuous at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} Hence for f to be continuous at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{4},f\left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}f(x) = \frac{{\sqrt 2 \cos x - 1}}{{\cot x - 1}},x \ne \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex}
|Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{\sqrt 2 \cos x - 1}}{{\cot x - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{\left( {\sqrt 2 \cos x - 1} \right)\sin x}}{{\cos x - \sin x}}{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{\left( {\sqrt 2 \cos x - 1} \right)}}{{\left( {\cos x - sinx} \right)}}.\frac{{\left( {\sqrt 2 \cos x + 1} \right)}}{{\left( {\sqrt 2 \cos x + 1} \right)}}.\frac{{\left( {\cos x + \sin x} \right)}}{{\left( {\cos x + \sin x} \right)}}.\sin x{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{2{{\cos }^2}x - 1}}{{{{\cos }^2}x - {{\sin }^2}x}}.\frac{{\cos x + \sin x}}{{\sqrt 2 \cos x + 1}}.\left( {\sin x} \right){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{\cos 2x}}{{\cos 2x}}.\left( {\frac{{\cos x + \sin x}}{{\sqrt 2 \cos x + 1}}} \right).\left( {\sin x} \right){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{\left( {\cos x + \sin x} \right)}}{{\sqrt 2 \cos x + 1}}\sin x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)}}{{\sqrt 2 .\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} + 1}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} f(x) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
If we define {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right) = \frac{1}{2},{/tex} then f(x) will become continuous at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex}
Hence for f to be continuous at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{4},f\left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Show that the function f given by {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}} - 1}}{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}} + 1}}\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} is discontinuous at x = 0.
question_html:
Show that the function f given by {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}} - 1}}{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}} + 1}}\,if\,\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
is discontinuous at x = 0.
solution_text: The right hand limit of f at x = 0 is given by {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}} - 1}}{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}} + 1}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{1 - \frac{1}{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}}}}{{1 + \frac{1}{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{1 - {e^{\frac{{ - 1}}{x}}}}}{{1 + {e^{\frac{{ - 1}}{x}}}}} = \frac{{1 - 0}}{{1 + 0}} = 1{/tex} Also,f(0)=0 Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ +}} f(x) \ne f(0){/tex} {tex}{/tex} . Hence f is discontinuous at x = 0.
solution_html:
The right hand limit of f at x = 0 is given by

 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}} - 1}}{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}} + 1}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{1 - \frac{1}{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}}}}{{1 + \frac{1}{{{e^{\frac{1}{x}}}}}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{1 - {e^{\frac{{ - 1}}{x}}}}}{{1 + {e^{\frac{{ - 1}}{x}}}}} = \frac{{1 - 0}}{{1 + 0}} = 1{/tex}

Also,f(0)=0

Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ +}} f(x) \ne f(0){/tex}  {tex}{/tex}. Hence f is discontinuous at x = 0.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Let {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}},\,\,if\,\,x < 0 \hfill \\ = a,\,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \frac{{\sqrt x }}{{\sqrt {16+ \sqrt x} - 4}},\,\,if\,x > 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} . For what value of a, f is continuous at x = 0?
question_html: Let  {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}},\,\,if\,\,x < 0 \hfill \\ = a,\,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \frac{{\sqrt x }}{{\sqrt {16+ \sqrt x} - 4}},\,\,if\,x > 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}. For what value of a, f is continuous at x = 0?
solution_text: Here f(0) = a . Left-hand limit of f at 0 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}2x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{2x \to {0^ - }} 8{\left( {\frac{{\sin 2x}}{{2x}}} \right)^2} = 8{\left( 1 \right)^2} = 8{/tex} and right hand limit of f at 0 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{\sqrt x }}{{\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x} - 4 }}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ +}} \frac{{\sqrt x \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right)}}{{\left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x} + 4} \right)\left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } - 4} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{\sqrt x \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right)}}{{{16} + \sqrt x - 16}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right) = 8{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = 8.{/tex} Hence f is continuous at x = 0 only if a = 8.
solution_html:
Here f(0) = a .

Left-hand limit of f at 0 is

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}2x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{2x \to {0^ - }} 8{\left( {\frac{{\sin 2x}}{{2x}}} \right)^2} = 8{\left( 1 \right)^2} = 8{/tex}

and right hand limit of f at 0 is

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{\sqrt x }}{{\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x} - 4 }}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ +}} \frac{{\sqrt x \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right)}}{{\left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x} + 4} \right)\left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } - 4} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{\sqrt x \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right)}}{{{16} + \sqrt x - 16}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right) = 8{/tex}

Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = 8.{/tex} 

Hence f is continuous at x = 0 only if a = 8.

question_text: Examine the differentiability of the function f defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {2 x+3, \text { if }-3 \leq x<-2}\\ {x+1, \text { if }-2 \leq x<0} \\ {x+2, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_html: Examine the differentiability of the function f defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {2 x+3, \text { if }-3 \leq x<-2}\\ {x+1, \text { if }-2 \leq x<0} \\ {x+2, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
solution_text: The only doubtful points for differentiability of f (x) are x = -2 and x = 0. Differentiability at x = -2. Now, Lf'(-2) {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(-2+h)-f(-2)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{2(-2+h)+3-(-2+1)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{2 h}{h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} 2=2{/tex} and Rf'(-2) {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{f(-2+h)-f(-2)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{-2+h+1-(-2+1)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{h-1-(-1)}{h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{h}{h}=1{/tex} Thus Rf'(-2) {tex}\neq{/tex} Lf'(-2). Therefore f is not differentiable at x = -2. Similarly, for differentiability at x = 0, we have {tex}f^{\prime}(0)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{0+h+1-(0+2)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{h-1}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0^{-}}\left(1-\frac{1}{h}\right){/tex} which does not exist. Hence f is not differentiable at x = 0.
solution_html:
The only doubtful points for differentiability of f (x) are x = -2 and x = 0.
Differentiability at x = -2.
Now, Lf'(-2) {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(-2+h)-f(-2)}{h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{2(-2+h)+3-(-2+1)}{h}{/tex}{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{2 h}{h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} 2=2{/tex}
and Rf'(-2) {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{f(-2+h)-f(-2)}{h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{-2+h+1-(-2+1)}{h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{h-1-(-1)}{h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{h}{h}=1{/tex}
Thus Rf'(-2) {tex}\neq{/tex} Lf'(-2). Therefore f is not differentiable at x = -2.
Similarly, for differentiability at x = 0, we have
{tex}f^{\prime}(0)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{0+h+1-(0+2)}{h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{h-1}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0^{-}}\left(1-\frac{1}{h}\right){/tex}
which does not exist. Hence f is not differentiable at x = 0.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} with respect to {tex}\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} , where {tex}x \in\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 1\right){/tex}
question_html: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} with respect to {tex}\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}, where {tex}x \in\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 1\right){/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} and {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} . We want to find {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}{/tex} Now {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} . Put x = {tex}\sin \theta \cdot\left(\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} Then u {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}{\sin \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}(\cot\theta){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)\right\}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1} x{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Now {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta})=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}\{\sin (\pi-2 \theta)\} \quad\left[\text { since } \frac{\pi}{2}<2 \theta<\pi\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-(\pi-2 \theta)=\frac{-\pi}{2}+2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{-\pi}{2}+2 \sin ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\frac{\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}{\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} and  {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}.
We want to find {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}{/tex}
Now {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex}. Put x = {tex}\sin \theta \cdot\left(\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
Then u {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}{\sin \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}(\cot\theta){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)\right\}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1} x{/tex}
Hence {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
Now {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta})=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}\{\sin (\pi-2 \theta)\} \quad\left[\text { since } \frac{\pi}{2}<2 \theta<\pi\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-(\pi-2 \theta)=\frac{-\pi}{2}+2 \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{-\pi}{2}+2 \sin ^{-1} x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
Hence {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\frac{\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}{\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}

question_text: Differential coefficient of sec (tan -1 x) w.r.t. x is
question_html: Differential coefficient of sec (tan-1x) w.r.t. x is

options.explanation: Complete step-by-step answer. Let us start with finding the derivative of sec tan -1 x. For finding the derivative, we will use the chain rule of differentiation. According to the chain rule of differentiation, we know {tex}\frac{d(f(g(x)))}{d x}=f^{\prime}(g(x)) \times g^{\prime}(x){/tex} . For {tex}\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right), f(x)=\sec x{/tex} and g(x) = tan -1 x We also Know that {tex}\frac{d(\sec x)}{d x}=\sec x \tan x{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d\left(\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)\right)}{d x}=\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) \tan \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) \times \frac{d\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)}{d x}{/tex} Now, we know that {tex}\tan ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} So, if we use this in our equation, we get {tex}\frac{d\left(\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)\right)}{d x}=\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) \tan \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) \times \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Now, according to the rules of inverse trigonometric function, we know tan {tex}\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right){/tex} = x. If we use this in our equation, we get {tex}\frac{d\left(\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)\right)}{d x}=\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) \tan \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) \times \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d\left(\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)\right)}{d x}=\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) \times \frac{x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Now, we are asked the differential coefficient of {tex}\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right){/tex} . So, from the above result, we can clearly see that the coefficient of {tex}\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right){/tex} in the derivative of {tex}\sec \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right) {/tex} is {tex} \frac{x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} .

question_text: If {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex} and {tex}v=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} , then {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}{/tex} is
question_html: If  {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex} and {tex}v=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex}, then {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}{/tex} is

options.explanation: Let {tex}u=\tan ^{-1} \frac{2 x}{1-x^{2}}{/tex} put {tex}{x}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} \mathbf{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {u}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {u}=\tan ^{-1}(\tan 2 \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {u}=2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = 2 tan -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Let v {tex}=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} Put x {tex}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathbf{v}=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathbf{v}=\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathbf{v}=2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathbf{v}=2 \tan ^{-1} \mathbf{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u}{d x} \times \frac{d x}{d v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}=\frac{2}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)} \times \frac{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}{2}=1{/tex}

question_text: The value of c in Rolle’s Theorem for the function f (x) = e x sinx, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} is
question_html: The value of c in Rolle’s Theorem for the function f (x) = ex sinx, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} is

options.explanation: Here, f(x) = e x sinx So, f '(x) = e x cosx + sinx e x Let c be the required point, then f '(c) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e c (sin c + cos c) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin c = - cosc {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan c = -1 = tan (- {tex}\frac{\pi}4{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}c = n \pi- \frac{\pi}4{/tex} for n = 1, the principal value of c is: {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}c=\frac{3\pi}4{/tex}

question_text: The value of c in Mean value theorem for the function f (x) = x (x - 2), x {tex}\in{/tex} [1, 2] is
question_html: The value of c in Mean value theorem for the function f (x) = x (x - 2), x {tex}\in{/tex} [1, 2] is

options.explanation: We have f'(c) = {tex}\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}{/tex} Here a = 1, b = 2 Put the value of c in f'(x) = 2x -2 Hence, 2c - 2 = {tex}\frac{f(2)-f(1)}{2-1}{/tex} = 2(2 - 2) - (-1) = 2(c - 1) = 1 c = 3\2

question_text: Match the following Column I Column II (a) if a function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {\frac{\sin 3 x}{x}, \text { if } x \neq 0} \\ {\frac{k}{2}, \quad \text { if } x=0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0, then k is equal to (i) {tex}|x|{/tex} (b) Every continuous function is differentiable (ii) True (c) An example of a function which is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at exactly one point (iii) 6 (d) The identity function i.e. f (x) {tex}=x ~~\forall~ x~ \in \mathbf{R}{/tex} is a continuous function (iv) False
question_html:
Match the following

Column I Column II
(a) if a function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {\frac{\sin 3 x}{x}, \text { if } x \neq 0} \\ {\frac{k}{2}, \quad \text { if } x=0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0, then k is equal to (i) {tex}|x|{/tex}
(b) Every continuous function is differentiable (ii) True
(c) An example of a function which is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at exactly one point (iii) 6
(d) The identity function i.e. f (x) {tex}=x ~~\forall~ x~ \in \mathbf{R}{/tex} is a continuous function (iv) False
